proposals.title: Local Symmetry Priors for Geometric Deep Learning: A Neural Collapse and Feature Evolution Perspective
proposals.motivation: Geometric deep learning often enforces global symmetries (e.g., SE(3)-equivariance) via specialized architectures, which can be overly rigid for complex real-world data like biomolecules or heterogeneous materials where relevant invariances are local and data-dependent. This mismatch limits model expressivity and efficiency. A principled framework for learning and imposing local symmetry constraints is lacking, representing a key gap between theoretical group-equivariant models and practical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that neural networks trained with objectives that prioritize the learning of local, data-dependent symmetry groups, rather than enforcing predefined global symmetries, will achieve superior generalization and feature efficiency on geometric tasks, and that this process can be analyzed through the lens of feature evolution and neural collapse dynamics.
proposals.method: We will design a novel architecture where each layer's feature transformation is conditioned on local geometric context (e.g., atomic environment), parameterizing a family of possible symmetry operations. This builds on Joan Bruna's work on feature evolution and conditionally defined models. Training will employ a two-term objective: a task-specific loss (e.g., for property prediction) plus a regularization term that encourages the learned per-instance transformations to form a locally consistent group structure, analyzed via their gradient flows. We will benchmark on molecular and point cloud datasets against strict equivariant networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new architectural paradigm that bridges rigid equivariant networks and fully unstructured models, offering a tractable theory for learned local symmetries. It will deliver concrete gains in data efficiency and generalization for geometric tasks, validated by a novel analysis linking local invariance learning to neural collapse phenomena in structured data.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is inverting the standard paradigm from enforcing global symmetries to learning local ones, framed through the specific theoretical lenses of neural collapse and conditional generative models that Bruna has recently explored, moving beyond a purely architectural focus to a training dynamics perspective.

proposals.title: Neural Collapse and Gradient Dynamics on Non-Smooth Metric Measure Spaces
proposals.motivation: Modern deep learning theory heavily relies on the assumption of smooth, differentiable manifold structures for data and latent spaces. However, critical applications involve data on highly irregular, discrete, or fractal geometric spaces (e.g., graphs, point clouds, combinatorial structures) that lack these properties, limiting the applicability of existing theoretical frameworks. A principled understanding of neural network training and feature evolution in such non-smooth settings is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the principles governing feature evolution and training dynamics, such as Neural Collapse, can be extended to non-smooth metric measure spaces by replacing differential geometric constructs with coarse geometric and optimal transport-based analogs. The resulting dynamics will exhibit universal phases that are robust to the lack of local smoothness.
proposals.method: We will develop a theoretical framework by modeling data domains as general metric measure spaces. We will analyze gradient flows for network parameters using tools from optimal transport (Wasserstein geometry) and coarse geometry, moving beyond Riemannian gradients. The analysis will focus on the evolution of features in Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and on single-index models with non-Gaussian data defined on irregular spaces, linking the geometry of the data domain to the Neural Collapse phenomenon observed in the final layer features.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new theoretical foundation for deep learning on irregular topological and geometric domains, directly applicable to GNNs and discrete generative models. It will yield practical insights into architecture design and training regularization for non-Euclidean data, bridging geometric deep learning with statistical learning theory.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in rigorously formalizing training dynamics without assuming a differentiable manifold structure, a significant departure from the prevailing analysis. This connects the geometric analysis of neural networks to the theory of analysis on metric spaces, a largely unexplored intersection.

proposals.title: Learning Latent Geometric Priors via Gradient Dynamics of Neural Feature Evolution
proposals.motivation: A core challenge in machine learning is to uncover the intrinsic geometric structure—such as a graph or manifold—on which high-dimensional data resides. Pre-2026, methods for graph learning from signals often rely on handcrafted similarity metrics or spectral assumptions, which can be brittle. This proposal addresses the inverse problem of directly inferring a latent geometry from observed signals, which is fundamental for improving generalization, robustness, and interpretability of models in non-Euclidean domains.
proposals.hypothesis: The gradient dynamics governing feature evolution in a trained neural network implicitly encode the latent geometric structure of the data. By analyzing these dynamics, particularly in the context of phenomena like Neural Collapse, we can recover an underlying graph or manifold without pre-specified similarity measures.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage neural framework. First, we train a graph neural network (GNN) or a single-index model on the observed signals, monitoring the evolution of features across layers during training. Second, we formulate an inverse problem where we optimize the parameters of a latent graph adjacency matrix such that the simulated gradient flow of the network's features, derived from the training dynamics, matches the observed evolution. This will leverage recent work on conditionally log-concave models to ensure well-posed optimization and connections to gradient flows on manifolds.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, trainable method for unsupervised geometry discovery that is directly coupled to the task-specific feature learning process. It will contribute a new theoretical perspective linking gradient-based training dynamics to latent structure inference, with practical implications for graph-based semi-supervised learning and generative modeling on unknown manifolds.
proposals.novelty: Unlike traditional spectral or similarity-based graph learning, our approach infers geometry from the internal learning dynamics of a neural network, making the discovered structure inherently adaptive to the downstream task. It uniquely combines the Neural Collapse perspective with inverse problem methodology for geometry recovery.

proposals.title: Neural Collapse and Efficient Feature Evolution in Geometric Deep Learning for Sensor Networks
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of sensor networks for environmental monitoring, smart cities, and IoT demands on-device intelligence, but deploying deep learning models on resource-constrained nodes remains a critical challenge. Pre-2026, geometric deep learning (GDL) has shown promise for graph-structured sensor data, yet standard GNNs are computationally heavy, limiting their use in low-power, low-memory settings where energy and bandwidth are severely limited.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Neural Collapse (NC) phenomenon—the convergence of last-layer features and classifiers to a simplex equiangular tight frame—can be leveraged to design ultra-lightweight GDL models that maintain strong discriminative power with drastically reduced parameters and operations, by enforcing efficient, structured feature evolution during training.
proposals.method: We will design a novel GDL architecture where the feature evolution across layers is explicitly constrained by principles derived from NC theory. The model will incorporate: 1) a highly sparse, geometrically-informed message-passing scheme based on local sensor topology, 2) a structured, low-rank final layer whose weights are initialized and regularized to approximate an ETF, and 3) a training objective combining task loss with a feature condensation term that encourages rapid convergence to a collapsed state, minimizing redundant computations. We will analyze the gradient dynamics between layers to prune unnecessary feature channels early in training.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new class of provably efficient GNNs for sensor networks, validated on real-world datasets like traffic flow or distributed temperature sensing. It will provide a theoretical bridge between the empirical Neural Collapse literature and practical model compression for non-Euclidean data, offering concrete design principles for resource-constrained geometric learning.
proposals.novelty: While NC has been studied in centralized image classification, its application to guide the architectural design and feature evolution in decentralized, geometric deep learning for sensor networks is novel. The integration of conditional log-concavity (from Bruna's prior work) to model uncertainties within this ultra-efficient framework is also unexplored.

proposals.title: Level Set Geometry and Conditioning: A Dynamical Systems View of Gradient Descent in Neural Networks
proposals.motivation: Understanding the convergence speed of gradient descent in deep learning remains a fundamental challenge. Pre-2026 work has linked conditioning to the Hessian's spectrum, but a direct geometric characterization via the loss function's level sets is less explored. This gap is critical because level set geometry dictates the local optimization landscape and the trajectory of gradient flow, directly impacting training dynamics and generalization.
proposals.hypothesis: The convergence rate of gradient descent for a given network architecture and dataset is governed by the anisotropic shape of the loss function's level sets in parameter space, which can be quantified via their curvature and eccentricity relative to the gradient flow direction.
proposals.method: We will analyze the evolution of feature representations in deep neural networks under gradient flow, building on Bruna's work on neural collapse and feature evolution. The core technical approach involves deriving differential equations for the motion of parameters along level sets, linking the rate of change of the gradient norm to the second fundamental form of these sets. We will employ tools from dynamical systems and Riemannian geometry, validated empirically by measuring level set geometry via local Hessian-vector products and correlating it with convergence speed in controlled settings (e.g., single-index models and simplified Graph Neural Networks).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel geometric framework for diagnosing optimization pathologies and predicting convergence rates. It will yield practical metrics for architecture design and initialization schemes that promote better-conditioned landscapes, moving beyond spectral analysis of the Hessian.
proposals.novelty: While conditioning is typically analyzed via the Hessian eigenvalues, this proposal directly connects the differential geometry of level sets to optimization dynamics, offering a trajectory-centric rather than a point-wise perspective. This links the geometry of the parameter space to the evolution of the internal feature representations studied in Bruna's prior work.

proposals.title: Level Set Regularity and Neural Collapse in Deep Residual Networks: A Scale Analysis of Feature Evolution
proposals.motivation: Understanding the training dynamics and feature evolution in very deep networks like ResNets remains a core challenge in deep learning theory. Pre-2026 work, including the Neural Collapse phenomenon and analysis of single index models, provides a framework for studying simplified settings, but lacks tools to analyze the complex, high-dimensional loss landscapes of modern architectures at scale. Bridging this gap is crucial for developing more principled training methods and architectures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the level set regularity algorithm, which analyzes the geometry of loss function sub-level sets, can be scaled and adapted to reveal a structured, hierarchical progression of feature representations in deep ResNets, connecting the dynamics of intermediate layers to the final Neural Collapse structure observed in the classifier.
proposals.method: We will adapt the level set regularity algorithm to operate on the activations of specific layers within standard ResNet architectures (e.g., ResNet-50, ResNet-152) trained on ImageNet. The method involves tracking the evolution of feature distributions within sub-level sets of the training loss across layers and training time. We will compute between-class and within-class covariance metrics, gradient alignment statistics, and the progression of the Neural Collapse metric (NC1) through the network depth, correlating these with the geometric properties of the loss level sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale empirical study linking the geometric analysis of loss landscapes to the empirical feature evolution in state-of-the-art deep networks. It will yield new diagnostic tools for understanding training stability and generalization in very deep models. The findings could inform the design of novel regularization techniques or architectural modifications grounded in the observed geometric properties.
proposals.novelty: While level set analysis has been applied in theoretical contexts, its application to empirically dissect feature evolution layer-by-layer in massive, practical architectures is novel. Combining this geometric tool with the Neural Collapse perspective creates a unique framework for scale analysis beyond the terminal phase of training.

proposals.title: Data Design for Convex Optimization Landscapes in Deep Linear and Shallow Nonlinear Networks
proposals.motivation: The non-convex loss landscapes of neural networks pose fundamental challenges for optimization and generalization. While prior work analyzes landscape properties for given data, we propose the inverse problem: given a fixed architecture, what data distributions guarantee favorable optimization geometry? This directly addresses the open question of how data structure interacts with architectural inductive bias to shape trainability.
proposals.hypothesis: For standard architectures like deep linear networks and single-hidden-layer ReLU networks, there exist non-Gaussian, structured data distributions—characterized by spectral and angular properties of their features—that enforce a connected, nearly convex loss landscape, enabling efficient gradient-based optimization from any initialization.
proposals.method: We will leverage tools from high-dimensional probability and random matrix theory to analyze the Hessian spectrum. For deep linear networks, we will characterize data covariance structures that ensure strict log-concavity of the loss, extending Bruna's prior work. For shallow nonlinear nets, we will employ a conditional strong log-concavity framework and single-index model analysis to derive sufficient conditions on the input distribution and target function. The core algorithm will be a generative procedure to synthesize or filter datasets meeting these spectral criteria, followed by empirical landscape visualization using mode connectivity and gradient descent trajectories.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel theoretical framework linking data geometry to optimization complexity, moving beyond worst-case analysis. It will yield practical guidelines for dataset construction and preprocessing to improve trainability. The results will also offer a new perspective on feature evolution in networks, connecting to the neural collapse phenomenon.
proposals.novelty: Inverting the classic analysis—designing data for a landscape, rather than analyzing the landscape for given data—is a fundamentally new direction. Combining tools from generative modeling, single-index models, and neural collapse to address this inverse problem is a unique synthesis of Bruna's recent research themes.

proposals.title: Inductive Biases from Physical Symmetries: A Constructive Framework for Constrained Neural Feature Learning
proposals.motivation: Despite their empirical success, deep neural networks often require vast amounts of data and lack interpretability, particularly in scientific domains where data is scarce and physical laws are known. Pre-2026, there is a growing interest in incorporating physical priors, but methods are often ad-hoc, applied post-hoc, or break the differentiability needed for end-to-end training. A systematic, constructive framework for embedding hard and soft physical constraints directly into network architectures is needed to improve data efficiency, generalization, and trustworthiness.
proposals.hypothesis: That a constructive, layer-wise design of neural networks, which explicitly enforces physical symmetries and regularities (e.g., conservation laws, Hamiltonian structure, scale separation) as architectural constraints, will lead to more data-efficient learning, more stable feature evolution, and better extrapolation beyond the training distribution compared to standard architectures.
proposals.method: We will develop a library of constrained neural layers that parameterize function spaces respecting specific physical invariants. This builds on Joan Bruna's work on feature evolution and gradient-based training. For Hamiltonian systems, we will design layers that inherently preserve symplectic structure. For scale-separated phenomena, we will construct multi-resolution architectures with built-in wavelet-like filters. For conservation laws, we will employ divergence-free parameterizations of weight matrices and activation functions. Training will involve analyzing the induced gradient dynamics on these constrained manifolds, linking to the 'Neural Collapse' perspective on feature structuring.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, open-source framework for physics-informed architecture design, moving beyond penalty-based methods. It will offer new theoretical insights into how hard constraints alter feature learning dynamics and neural collapse phenomena in structured domains. The resulting models will be validated on benchmark PDEs and single-index models with non-Gaussian data, demonstrating superior sample complexity.
proposals.novelty: Unlike common Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) that add soft constraint losses, our approach enforces constraints architecturally, ensuring they are satisfied exactly. Furthermore, we connect this constructive design to the theoretical study of feature learning trajectories and gradient flow, a perspective largely absent from prior physics-guided ML work.

proposals.title: Distributed Training of Equivariant Neural Networks via Stochastic Gradient Symmetrization
proposals.motivation: Geometric models, such as equivariant neural networks, are essential for learning on symmetric datasets from physics, chemistry, and social networks. However, scaling their training to massive graphs or manifolds is hindered by computational constraints and the need to preserve symmetry during distributed optimization. Pre-2026 distributed methods (e.g., data parallelism) are not designed for the structured parameter sharing and gradient dependencies inherent in these models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a distributed training framework enforcing stochastic gradient symmetrization at each optimization step can achieve linear scaling while preserving the exact equivariance of the learned model, even under asynchronous updates.
proposals.method: We will develop a new distributed optimization protocol tailored to group-equivariant architectures. The core algorithm will involve partitioning the symmetric input domain (e.g., graph orbits or manifold patches) across workers. Each worker computes local gradients on its partition, which are then processed by a symmetrization layer—inspired by the feature evolution analysis in Neural Collapse—before a consensus step. This ensures the aggregated gradient respects the global symmetry, preventing feature drift. We will integrate this with gradient checkpointing for memory-intensive layers and test it on large-scale graph neural networks and rotation-equivariant CNNs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, scalable training method for geometric deep learning, enabling the application of sophisticated equivariant models to previously intractable dataset sizes. It will contribute new theory on the convergence of symmetrized stochastic gradient descent in distributed settings, bridging the gap between symmetry-preserving architectures and large-scale systems.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard model parallelism, our method explicitly maintains geometric constraints during distributed gradient aggregation, a challenge not addressed by pre-2026 distributed deep learning frameworks. The integration of stochastic symmetrization with asynchronous optimization for equivariant networks is a novel direction.

proposals.title: Inverse Group Discovery from Equivariant Networks: A Spectral and Geometric Approach
proposals.motivation: Equivariant neural networks, which encode known symmetries like rotations or permutations, are central to modern deep learning for their data efficiency and generalization. However, in many real-world settings, the full symmetry group of the data is unknown or only partially specified. The inverse problem—inferring the underlying symmetry group directly from a trained equivariant network’s weights and feature representations—remains unsolved. This capability would automate the discovery of inductive biases and validate architectural assumptions, bridging geometric deep learning and unsupervised structure learning.
proposals.hypothesis: The weight structure and feature evolution dynamics of a trained equivariant network contain sufficient spectral and geometric signatures to uniquely identify, up to isomorphism, the data symmetry group that informed its training.
proposals.method: We will analyze the network's linear equivariant layers via their parameterization as intertwiners between group representations. Using spectral tools from non-commutative harmonic analysis, we will decompose the weight tensors to recover the irreducible representations (irreps) present. Concurrently, we will study the feature distributions across layers, extending the Neural Collapse analysis to group orbits, to detect invariant separability and orbit structure. The method will combine algebraic constraints from the weight space with statistical tests on feature correlations to hypothesize a candidate group, which will be validated through a novel gradient-based meta-learning procedure that perturbs the network to test group closure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic framework for solving the inverse symmetry discovery problem, yielding a practical algorithm to extract a symmetry group hypothesis from a pre-trained model. It will contribute new theoretical connections between the representation theory of networks and the geometry of feature learning, extending the neural collapse perspective to structured groups. The tools developed will enable better model interpretation and automated symmetry discovery for scientific domains.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on building networks for known groups, we invert this process to *discover* the group from the network, a non-trivial inverse problem. Our approach uniquely combines spectral analysis of equivariant weights with a dynamical, feature-based collapse analysis, moving beyond simple weight visualization to a principled recovery method.

proposals.title: Task-Driven Stability in Deep Networks: Learning Invariance to Relevant Deformations via Constrained Feature Evolution
proposals.motivation: Deep learning representations often seek stability (invariance) to a broad, generic set of input deformations, which can discard discriminative signal crucial for specific tasks. This is particularly evident in domains like fine-grained visual classification or non-Gaussian data settings, where class-specific deformations are informative. A principled framework to learn representations invariant only to a task-relevant subset of deformations is lacking, limiting both efficiency and discriminative power.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling and constraining the feature evolution during training, we can learn representations that are invariant only to a learned, task-relevant group of deformations, thereby improving generalization and sample efficiency compared to models enforcing broad, indiscriminate invariance.
proposals.method: We will formulate this as a constrained optimization problem on the feature manifold. Building on the Neural Collapse perspective for feature evolution and single-index models for non-Gaussian data, we will introduce a learnable, task-dependent subgroup of the symmetry group acting on the input. A regularization term, derived from the gradient of network functions with respect to these subgroup actions, will penalize invariance to irrelevant deformations while promoting it for relevant ones. Training will involve alternating steps of updating network parameters and refining the estimated relevant subgroup via a secondary objective based on class separation metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel theoretical and algorithmic framework for task-adaptive invariance in deep learning. It will yield concrete architectures that outperform standard invariant models on tasks requiring discrimination within deformation families (e.g., fine-grained classification, graph isomorphism detection). We will also contribute an analysis linking feature evolution dynamics to learned symmetry groups.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on invariance which assumes the relevant symmetry group is known a priori (e.g., translations for CNNs), we propose to *learn* the appropriate, potentially small, invariance subgroup directly from the task and data. This challenges the prevailing paradigm of enforcing stability to all deformations within a pre-defined, broad class.

proposals.title: Stable Feature Initialization via Scattering Transforms: A Study of Training Dynamics and Neural Collapse in Hybrid Deep Networks
proposals.motivation: Deep convolutional networks are powerful but their training dynamics are sensitive to initialization and can be data-hungry. The scattering transform, pioneered by Bruna and Mallat, provides stable, geometry-preserving representations but lacks adaptability. Integrating these approaches could yield models with improved sample efficiency and robustness, addressing a core challenge in supervised learning.
proposals.hypothesis: Using a fixed, non-trainable scattering transform as the first layer of a deep convolutional network will stabilize early training dynamics, accelerate the convergence of feature representations, and induce a more structured evolution of deep features towards a neural collapse configuration, especially in limited-data regimes.
proposals.method: We will construct hybrid architectures where the first layer is a predefined scattering network (using Morlet wavelets) computing invariant coefficients, followed by a standard deep convolutional network (e.g., ResNet). We will train these models on standard vision datasets (CIFAR, ImageNet) and analyze the gradient flow and feature evolution using metrics from Neural Collapse theory. A key comparison will be against standard CNN baselines, measuring convergence speed, robustness to label noise, and the geometry of the final layer features.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled framework for hybridizing geometric priors with learned representations. It will yield new insights into how stable initial features affect the entire training trajectory and final feature geometry, potentially leading to more data-efficient and interpretable models. The analysis will bridge the scattering transform literature with modern studies of deep network training dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While scattering networks and deep CNNs have been studied separately, a systematic analysis of their integrated training dynamics, particularly through the lens of feature evolution and Neural Collapse, is unexplored. The proposal moves beyond using scattering as a mere pre-processor to treating it as an integral, theoretically grounded component whose impact on end-to-end gradient-based learning is rigorously studied.

proposals.title: Neural Compression for Quantization: A Generative Model Approach to Dictionary Learning
proposals.motivation: Quantization is crucial for deploying deep neural networks on resource-constrained devices, but traditional dictionary-based methods (e.g., k-means, product quantization) often rely on simplistic geometric assumptions that fail to capture the complex, multi-modal structure of neural network features. This mismatch leads to suboptimal compression and accuracy loss. Recent advances in generative models, particularly those learning conditionally strongly log-concave distributions, offer a principled framework for modeling feature spaces more accurately.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing static, geometric dictionary learning with a learned neural compression scheme based on conditional generative models will yield a more expressive and adaptive quantization process, leading to superior rate-distortion-accuracy trade-offs in neural network compression.
proposals.method: We will formulate quantization as learning a conditional generative model p(x|z), where x is a feature vector (e.g., from a pre-trained network layer) and z is a discrete latent code. Inspired by Bruna's work on conditionally log-concave models, we will design an encoder-decoder architecture where the encoder performs amortized variational inference to map x to a discrete codebook, and the decoder reconstructs x. Training will minimize a loss combining reconstruction error (distortion) and a regularization term on the code distribution (rate), using straight-through gradient estimators for the discrete bottleneck. We will analyze the feature evolution during this compression, extending the neural collapse perspective to the quantized setting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, generative framework for neural network quantization that moves beyond Euclidean assumptions. It will deliver a practical compression algorithm with improved performance and a theoretical analysis linking feature evolution in trained networks to optimal compression under generative priors.
proposals.novelty: While neural compression has been explored for images, its application to quantizing the internal *features* of a neural network for deployment is novel. The key innovation is the integration of strong generative priors (like conditional log-concavity) into the quantization process, creating an adaptive 'dictionary' that evolves with the feature statistics, unlike fixed codebooks.

proposals.title: Substructure-Aware Super-Resolution via Continuous-Feature Graph Neural Networks
proposals.motivation: Real-time image super-resolution on mobile devices requires models that are both extremely efficient and perceptually accurate. Pre-2026, lightweight CNNs sacrifice structural coherence for speed, while methods that model long-range dependencies (e.g., via attention or graphs) are computationally prohibitive. A fundamental gap exists in efficiently capturing the nuanced, non-local relationships between image patches that are crucial for high-quality upscaling.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling an image as a graph with nodes/edges carrying continuous feature vectors (e.g., texture, gradient descriptors) and designing a GNN that performs efficient, approximate substructure counting, we can learn a super-resolution model that achieves superior perceptual fidelity at real-time speeds on constrained hardware.
proposals.method: We will construct a graph from an image where nodes are patches and edges connect perceptually similar patches based on continuous feature distances. We will design a lightweight, message-passing GNN architecture whose layers are informed by fast, approximate algorithms for weighting small graph motifs (e.g., triangles, 4-cycles). This co-design integrates substructure-aware aggregation with feature evolution, drawing on neural collapse principles to ensure discriminative feature learning. The model will be trained end-to-end using a loss combining pixel-wise accuracy and a perceptual metric, with optimization focusing on gradient flow between the substructure weighting and the final super-resolution output.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, deployable super-resolution model that sets a new state-of-the-art in speed-quality trade-off for mobile platforms. Theoretically, it will advance the understanding of feature evolution in GNNs applied to non-discrete graph data and provide a framework for integrating combinatorial substructure awareness into differentiable neural architectures.
proposals.novelty: The approach fundamentally relaxes the discrete-attribute assumption of prior graph-based image processing, and its co-design of approximate substructure algorithms with neural feature learning is a non-obvious fusion of combinatorial reasoning and deep learning for efficiency.

proposals.title: Beyond Discriminative Equivalence: A Study of Neural Network Feature Dynamics in Generative and Graph Reconstruction Tasks
proposals.motivation: Recent theoretical work, including Neural Collapse, has focused on understanding feature evolution in neural networks primarily for classification. This provides a limited view of network behavior, as modern architectures are equally critical for generative modeling and structured data tasks like graph reconstruction. A unified theory of feature learning must extend beyond discriminative power to explain dynamics in these broader settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the equivalence of feature evolution across different architectures and training regimes, observed in classification, does not hold for generative or graph reconstruction tasks, where the training dynamics are governed by different statistical and geometric constraints.
proposals.method: We will analyze feature evolution dynamics in two parallel settings: 1) Training conditional generative models (e.g., conditional VAEs or score-based models) on synthetic data with known log-concave distributions, tracking the layer-wise feature statistics and their relation to the data manifold. 2) Training Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for link prediction or graph generation, extending the Neural Collapse perspective to the pairwise interactions between nodes. We will employ tools from dynamical systems and measure deviations from the simple, symmetric structures seen in classification.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic comparison of feature learning dynamics across discriminative, generative, and structured prediction tasks. It will identify task-specific invariants and offer a more complete theoretical framework for understanding deep network training, with implications for architecture design and regularization.
proposals.novelty: While feature dynamics are well-studied for classification, their systematic analysis under generative and graph-structured losses is novel. The proposal directly challenges the assumption that insights from discriminative tasks generalize, leveraging Bruna's expertise in generative models and GNN analysis.

proposals.title: Neural Collapse and Gradient Flows for Inverse Graph Generation via Universal Approximators
proposals.motivation: Generative models for structured data, particularly graphs, are crucial for drug discovery and social network analysis, but most pre-2026 methods (e.g., GANs, VAEs) are direct and lack theoretical grounding for the inverse problem: constructing graphs from desired properties. This gap limits controllable generation. Joan Bruna's recent work on feature evolution and neural collapse in GNNs provides a novel lens to study the training dynamics of such inverse models.
proposals.hypothesis: That the training dynamics of a universal approximator (e.g., an MLP or GNN) trained to solve the inverse graph property problem will exhibit a neural collapse-like phenomenon, where the internal representations of graphs with similar target properties converge, and that this geometric structure can be harnessed to improve generation fidelity and efficiency.
proposals.method: We will frame graph generation as learning a mapping from a target property vector (e.g., spectral gap, degree distribution) to a probability distribution over adjacency matrices. A neural network (the universal approximator) will be trained using gradient-based methods to minimize a loss between the properties of its generated graphs and the targets. We will specifically analyze the gradient flow dynamics of the network's penultimate layer features, drawing from Bruna's neural collapse analysis for GNNs, to understand and potentially regularize the feature evolution during training. Training will leverage techniques from conditionally strongly log-concave generative models to ensure stable sampling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new theoretical framework for inverse graph generation based on training dynamics and neural collapse, moving beyond purely architectural innovations. It will yield a practical, more interpretable generation method with controllable properties, validated on standard graph benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in explicitly connecting the theory of neural collapse and feature evolution—typically studied for classification—to the inverse problem of generative modeling for structured, non-Euclidean data like graphs, offering a dynamics-centric perspective rather than a static architectural one.

proposals.title: Neural Collapse and Feature Synthesis in the One-Shot Learning Limit
proposals.motivation: One-shot learning is a critical challenge for deploying neural networks in data-scarce domains like medical imaging or rare event analysis. Pre-2026, theoretical frameworks for understanding feature learning, such as Neural Collapse, have been studied in the many-sample regime, but their behavior and utility in the extreme limit of a single support example per class remain unexplored. This project aims to bridge this gap, providing a principled, theoretically grounded approach to an empirically difficult problem.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the geometric and dynamical principles underlying Neural Collapse can be adapted to guide the synthesis of effective feature representations and training dynamics, even when only a single example is available, by leveraging strong structural priors on the data distribution.
proposals.method: We will develop a specialized training framework based on conditionally strongly log-concave generative models (inspired by Bruna's prior work) to act as a structured data prior. This model will be integrated into a meta-learning loop where the inner loop performs rapid adaptation from the single example, and the outer loop optimizes the model's initial features and the generative prior's parameters. We will analyze the resulting feature evolution and gradient dynamics, drawing on tools from the analysis of Single Index Models and Graph Neural Networks to characterize the learning process in this extreme regime.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel theoretical perspective on one-shot learning through the lens of feature dynamics and collapse phenomena. It will yield a practical algorithm that explicitly optimizes for sample efficiency by synthesizing informative features from a strong prior, moving beyond purely metric-based or black-box meta-learning approaches prevalent pre-2026.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in rigorously connecting the Neural Collapse theory—typically a many-sample phenomenon—to the one-shot regime and in using a trainable, conditionally log-concave generative model as an integral, optimizable component of the feature learning pipeline for extreme data efficiency.

proposals.title: Learning with Latent Graph Dynamics: A Neural Collapse and Gradient Flow Analysis
proposals.motivation: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) predominantly assume a fixed, known graph structure, which is a major limitation for real-world applications like social networks, biological interactions, and sensor networks where relational structures evolve or are partially observed. This static assumption restricts model applicability and fails to capture the co-evolution of features and structure during training, a dynamic central to representation learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling the graph structure as a latent, sample-dependent variable that co-evolves with node features during training will lead to improved generalization and induce a more structured feature geometry, measurable through the lens of neural collapse on the learned latent graphs.
proposals.method: We will develop a joint optimization framework where node features are updated via standard GNN gradient steps, while the graph adjacency is parameterized as a function of features via a differentiable, sparsity-promoting mechanism (e.g., a learned threshold on kernel-based similarities). The training will minimize a combined loss on node classification and a regularization term for graph smoothness. We will analyze the coupled gradient flow dynamics of features and the adjacency matrix, extending recent neural collapse analysis for GNNs to this adaptive setting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method for training GNNs with uncertain or dynamic relational structure. It will yield new theoretical insights into the interplay between feature evolution and graph learning dynamics, contributing to both the theory of deep learning on graphs and practical algorithm design.
proposals.novelty: While graph structure learning exists, the novelty lies in rigorously analyzing the *joint training dynamics* of features and graph structure through the framework of gradient flow and neural collapse, moving beyond static or alternating optimization schemes common pre-2026.

proposals.title: Joint Graph Signal and Architecture Learning via Bilevel Optimization
proposals.motivation: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are typically applied to fixed, pre-defined graph structures, which may be noisy, incomplete, or suboptimal for the downstream task. This limits performance in applications like molecular property prediction or recommendation systems where the relational structure is part of the modeling problem. A method to co-adapt the input graph signals and the network architecture could unlock more powerful and data-efficient relational models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that jointly optimizing the initial node features (graph signals) and the parameters of a GNN through a bilevel optimization framework will lead to learned graph representations that are more aligned with the task, thereby improving generalization and robustness compared to using fixed or separately learned inputs.
proposals.method: We will formulate a bilevel optimization problem where the outer loop learns an initial graph signal matrix (or a parametric generator for it) and the inner loop trains a GNN on these signals for a supervised task. The gradient of the validation loss with respect to the signal parameters will be computed using implicit differentiation through the GNN's training dynamics, extending recent work on implicit meta-learning. We will instantiate this with architectures from the researcher's recent work, such as analyzing the feature evolution and neural collapse phenomena in the resulting jointly trained system.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, end-to-end framework for graph representation learning that blurs the line between data and architecture. It will yield new theoretical insights into the co-evolution of features and parameters in GNNs and deliver a practical algorithm with demonstrated improvements on benchmark node and graph classification tasks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored learning graph structure, jointly and differentially learning the continuous-valued node feature signals alongside the network parameters from scratch for a downstream task is a distinct and largely unexplored direction, moving beyond fixed or discretely perturbed inputs.

proposals.title: A Global Prior for Manifold Learning: Reconstruction from a Single Implicit Neural Network
proposals.motivation: Manifold learning and generative modeling often rely on stitching together local charts or patches, which introduces complexity and can fail to capture coherent global structure. A single, scalable neural network representing an entire data manifold as a unified prior would provide a more elegant and potentially more powerful framework for tasks like out-of-distribution generalization, robust interpolation, and analysis of feature evolution. This aligns with the geometric perspective in deep learning but pushes beyond local approximations.
proposals.hypothesis: A sufficiently expressive implicit neural representation (INR) can be trained to serve as a global prior for a high-dimensional data manifold, enabling high-fidelity reconstruction and generation from a single network, and its training dynamics will exhibit a form of neural collapse across the continuous latent space.
proposals.method: We will formulate the problem as learning a single INR, f_θ: Z → X, mapping from a low-dimensional latent space Z to the data manifold in ambient space X. Inspired by recent work on conditionally strongly log-concave models and single index models, we will design a novel training objective combining a reconstruction loss with a spectral regularization on the Jacobian of f_θ to enforce local isometry and prevent rank collapse. The training will utilize a contrastive or score-based gradient method to shape the latent space, and we will analyze the evolution of features across network layers using tools from our prior work on neural collapse in GNNs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for global manifold learning via a single network, moving beyond patch-based methods. It will contribute a new analysis of feature learning dynamics in continuous latent spaces and deliver a practical, scalable model for tasks like data completion and generative modeling with a globally consistent prior.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the shift from a collection of local charts to a single global implicit neural representation as a manifold prior, coupled with an analysis linking its training dynamics to neural collapse phenomena. This integrates geometric deep learning with modern generative modeling and feature evolution theory in a non-standard way.

proposals.title: Neutral Initialization: Testing the Necessity of Structured Priors in Convolutional Neural Networks
proposals.motivation: The initialization of convolutional filters is a critical design choice, with structured approaches like wavelet bases often advocated as a strong, physically-motivated prior for learning multiscale features. This mirrors a pre-paradigm in evolutionary biology before Neutral Theory, where all variation was assumed to be adaptive. A rigorous null model is needed to test if such structure is truly necessary or if optimization can discover effective features from simpler, less constrained starting points.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that, analogous to genetic drift in Neutral Theory, the optimization dynamics of stochastic gradient descent can overcome the lack of a multiscale prior, making the performance of randomly initialized CNNs statistically indistinguishable from those with wavelet-structured initialization after sufficient training.
proposals.method: We will conduct controlled experiments comparing standard random (e.g., He/Kaiming) and wavelet-based initializations across architectures (e.g., VGG, ResNet) and datasets (CIFAR-10/100, ImageNet). We will measure final test accuracy, convergence dynamics, and the evolution of feature manifolds using metrics like Neural Collapse and layer-wise feature similarity. Statistical significance will be assessed via multiple runs, and we will analyze the gradient flow to characterize the 'neutral drift' of weights during training.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, biologically-inspired framework for evaluating architectural priors, potentially simplifying initialization schemes. It will contribute a deeper theoretical understanding of the interplay between initialization, optimization, and feature learning, moving beyond heuristic design rules.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in formally adapting the conceptual framework of evolutionary biology's Neutral Theory to deep learning, establishing a rigorous null model for initialization that challenges a widely held design principle. This cross-disciplinary analogy provides a new lens to separate the effects of prior structure from the capacity of optimization.

proposals.title: Implicit Sufficient Statistics: Emergent Feature Extraction via Pruning in Neural Networks Trained on Multi-Modal Data
proposals.motivation: Learning from multi-modal distributions, common in real-world data, often relies on pre-specified sufficient statistics or mixture models, which can be restrictive or misspecify the underlying structure. Recent theoretical work, such as on conditionally strongly log-concave models, highlights the importance of understanding how generative models capture complex data geometry. This project seeks a more flexible, data-driven alternative inspired by biological sensory systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Training a neural network with sparsity-inducing constraints on multi-modal data will result in a pruned architecture whose surviving connectivity and feature activations implicitly encode the statistically sufficient features for discriminating between modes, without requiring their explicit definition.
proposals.method: We will train a fully-connected or convolutional network on synthetic and real-world multi-modal datasets (e.g., mixtures, graph-structured data). We will impose structured sparsity via iterative magnitude pruning or L1/L0 regularization during training, as studied in the neural collapse and feature evolution literature. The dynamics of feature learning and network connectivity will be analyzed using gradient-based metrics and layer-wise activation statistics to trace how pruning shapes the emergent internal representations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, architecture-centric perspective on sufficient statistics for multi-modal learning, linking pruning dynamics to implicit feature extraction. It will offer practical insights for designing efficient networks that automatically discover discriminative features, with potential applications in robust representation learning for graph neural networks and beyond-Gaussian single-index models.
proposals.novelty: The approach departs from explicit statistical modeling by using architectural sparsity as a mechanistic driver for emergent feature discovery, directly translating a neuroscience analogy of sparse coding into a structured deep learning framework for multi-modal distributions.

proposals.title: Variance-Adaptive Warm-up: A Plug-in Method for Stabilizing and Accelerating Optimizer Convergence
proposals.motivation: Warm-up schedules for learning rates are a critical but poorly understood heuristic in modern deep learning, used to prevent early training instability. Current fixed schedules waste computation and remain sensitive to hyperparameters, lacking a principled, adaptive foundation. This work addresses the gap between heuristic practice and the theoretical understanding of gradient dynamics at initialization.
proposals.hypothesis: That directly estimating and adapting to the gradient variance in the initial training phase provides a more stable and efficient warm-up regime than fixed schedules, leading to faster convergence and reduced hyperparameter sensitivity.
proposals.method: We will develop a Variance-Adaptive Warm-up (VAW) module that, during the initial training steps, maintains a running estimate of the per-parameter gradient variance. This estimate will be used to compute a variance-adaptive scaling factor for the base learning rate, ensuring the effective step size remains within a stable region defined by a theoretical bound on expected loss change. The module will be implemented as a lightweight, optimizer-agnostic wrapper compatible with PyTorch and TensorFlow, requiring minimal code changes.
proposals.contribution: This work provides a principled, plug-and-play replacement for heuristic warm-up, backed by a novel stability analysis. It will deliver practical benefits in training efficiency and robustness across architectures, advancing the systematic study of training dynamics. The method and analysis will be made publicly available as an open-source library.
proposals.novelty: While gradient variance is known to be high at initialization, its direct, online estimation for real-time learning rate control during warm-up is unexplored. This moves beyond static schedules and simple decay rules to a feedback-driven, theoretically grounded adaptive mechanism.

proposals.title: Implicit Statistical Preconditioning via Co-Adaptive Neural Surrogates
proposals.motivation: Training deep generative models on complex, multi-modal data often relies on explicit sufficient statistics, which can be computationally prohibitive or ill-defined for modern, high-dimensional distributions. This bottleneck limits scalability and stability, particularly in methods like score-based diffusion or energy-based models where gradient updates depend on these statistics. A more efficient, implicit approach is needed to capture essential statistical features without explicit calculation overhead.
proposals.hypothesis: A small, concurrently trained neural surrogate can implicitly estimate sufficient statistics, and its integration as an adaptive preconditioner in the main model's gradient updates will yield a more stable and computationally efficient optimization trajectory.
proposals.method: We will design a lightweight convolutional or transformer network that ingests mini-batch data and outputs surrogate sufficient statistics. This network will be co-trained with the primary model (e.g., a generative neural network) using a coupled objective that includes both the primary loss and a consistency loss ensuring the surrogate's outputs guide effective preconditioning. Specifically, we will modify gradient-based updates to use the surrogate's output as a preconditioning matrix, drawing on connections to natural gradient and adaptive optimization. The training dynamics and feature evolution of this coupled system will be analyzed through the lens of Neural Collapse and related geometric frameworks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for efficient, stable training of complex models by replacing explicit statistical computations with learned, implicit estimators. It will contribute new theory on the co-adaptation dynamics between a model and its neural preconditioner, extending analysis of feature evolution in networks. The resulting method aims to offer practical speed and memory improvements for training on multi-modal data distributions.
proposals.novelty: While preconditioning and surrogate models exist separately, their tight integration—where a neural surrogate for statistics is co-adapted end-to-end to precondition the main model's training—is unexplored. This moves beyond using fixed or explicitly calculated statistics to a fully adaptive, context-aware estimation process.

proposals.title: Stable and Efficient Training of Conditionally Log-Concave Models via Adaptive Gradient Flow and Neural Collapse Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Training deep generative models, such as conditionally strongly log-concave models, often suffers from instability and slow convergence due to complex, high-dimensional loss landscapes and the difficulty of aligning feature representations across network layers. This inefficiency limits the scalability and practical application of these powerful models. Addressing these training dynamics is crucial for advancing robust and computationally feasible generative modeling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling and regularizing the feature evolution dynamics during training—inspired by the Neural Collapse phenomenon—within an adaptive gradient flow framework will significantly improve the convergence rate and stability of training for conditionally log-concave generative models.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel training algorithm that integrates two key components. First, we will derive an adaptive preconditioner for the gradient flow, informed by the local geometry of the conditional log-concave loss, to accelerate convergence. Second, we will introduce a regularization term that penalizes deviations from a structured feature evolution trajectory, encouraging the within-class feature variability to collapse towards their class means as training progresses, akin to Neural Collapse in classifiers. This will be implemented and tested on the generative model framework from Bruna's prior work on conditionally log-concave models.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, principled training algorithm that demonstrably improves the efficiency and robustness of training for a class of generative models. It will provide a deeper theoretical and empirical understanding of how feature evolution regularization interacts with gradient-based optimization, bridging concepts from generative modeling and deep learning theory.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic application of Neural Collapse—a phenomenon studied primarily in classification—as a regularizer for stabilizing the feature learning process in generative model training, combined with a geometrically adaptive gradient flow specifically tailored for conditionally log-concave objectives.

proposals.title: Implicit Sufficiency in Multi-Modal Learning: Architectural Biases for Information-Preserving Fusion
proposals.motivation: Multi-modal fusion models often lack theoretical grounding, leading to fragile representations that discard task-critical information or overfit to spurious correlations. Classical statistics offers the principle of sufficiency for optimal data reduction, but computing explicit sufficient statistics for complex, high-dimensional data (e.g., images, graphs, text) is intractable. This project aims to bridge this gap by using deep network architecture itself as a mechanism to enforce sufficiency implicitly, addressing a core challenge in robust representation learning.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific architectural inductive biases—in the form of constrained connectivity, multi-modal bottlenecks, and gradient alignment objectives—can compel a neural network to learn a fused representation that is provably sufficient for a downstream task, without ever computing the sufficient statistic explicitly.
proposals.method: We will design a hybrid CNN/Transformer fusion module with a structured bottleneck. The architecture will enforce modality-invariant feature extraction via weight-sharing in early CNN layers, followed by cross-modal attention. A key technical component is a novel regularization loss, derived from the gradient of the task loss with respect to the raw input modalities, designed to minimize the Fisher information loss between the input and the fused representation. We will analyze the evolving feature distributions, drawing on Neural Collapse theory, to empirically and theoretically validate the sufficiency of the learned representation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new architectural framework for principled multi-modal fusion with an implicit sufficiency guarantee. It will yield concrete design principles for building more robust and interpretable models that are less prone to discarding salient information. The theoretical connection between architectural bias and statistical sufficiency will advance the understanding of deep network training dynamics.
proposals.novelty: Instead of approximating explicit sufficient statistics, we propose to enforce sufficiency implicitly through network architecture and training dynamics, a departure from both classical statistical and standard deep learning approaches. The integration of gradient-based regularization with structural bottlenecks to control information flow is a novel synthesis of ideas from optimization and statistical theory.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning Adversarial Priors for Fast Adaptation to Unseen Data Degradations
proposals.motivation: Generative models, such as Gibbs models, are powerful for image reconstruction under known degradations (e.g., blur, noise). However, their performance degrades significantly on novel, unseen corruption types, requiring costly retraining or fine-tuning. A model that can rapidly adapt to arbitrary, unseen degradations using minimal data would be transformative for real-world imaging applications where corruption models are imperfectly known.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by meta-learning a Gibbs model generator and a discriminator jointly across a distribution of degradation tasks, the generator will internalize robust, corruption-invariant priors and adaptive update rules, enabling rapid few-shot adaptation to novel degradations that outperform models trained with standard adversarial or meta-learning alone.
proposals.method: We will frame different degradation operators (e.g., Gaussian blur, JPEG compression, sensor noise) as distinct tasks. We will implement a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) outer loop that learns initial parameters for a neural Gibbs model (the generator) and a convolutional discriminator. The inner loop will consist of a few steps of an adversarial training objective (e.g., Wasserstein GAN loss) specific to a sampled degradation. The meta-update will optimize for initial parameters such that after a few inner-loop adversarial steps on a new task, the generator produces high-fidelity reconstructions that fool its co-adapted discriminator.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a unified framework for degradation-agnostic image reconstruction, demonstrating superior few-shot adaptation on benchmark corruption datasets. It will provide new theoretical and empirical insights into how adversarial dynamics within a meta-learning context shape the learned feature representations and gradient-based adaptation pathways of generative models.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the tight integration of the adversarial training loop *inside* the meta-learning optimization, forcing the generator to learn not just to reconstruct but to strategically counter a co-evolving adversary across tasks, which is distinct from sequential or independent applications of these techniques.

proposals.title: Adversarial Fine-Tuning of Conditionally Log-Concave Gibbs Models via Spectral Regularization
proposals.motivation: Conditionally Strongly Log-Concave (CSLC) generative models, as studied by Bruna et al., offer theoretical guarantees but can suffer from suboptimal fine-tuning and mode coverage in practice. Pre-2026, Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) have developed sophisticated adversarial training techniques to improve sample fidelity and diversity, yet these methods are largely untested within the structured, theoretically grounded framework of CSLC Gibbs distributions. Bridging this gap could yield more powerful and practical generative models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating adversarial training objectives and spectral normalization—a key GAN stabilization technique—into the fine-tuning phase of a CSLC Gibbs model will significantly improve its sample quality and mode coverage compared to standard maximum likelihood training, without compromising its conditional log-concavity properties.
proposals.method: We will base our generative model on the established CSLC Gibbs framework. For fine-tuning, we will introduce a critic network, trained with a Wasserstein or hinge adversarial loss, to distinguish between fine-tuned model samples and target data. The critic's layers will be spectrally normalized to enforce Lipschitz constraints, ensuring stable training. The gradient from the critic will then be combined with the standard maximum likelihood gradient to update the Gibbs model's parameters, effectively using adversarial feedback to guide the fine-tuning process beyond the initial log-concave basin.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel hybrid training paradigm that marries the theoretical stability of conditionally log-concave models with the empirical sample quality gains of modern adversarial training. It will yield a concrete algorithm for robust generative model fine-tuning and offer new insights into the feature evolution and neural collapse dynamics within the adversarial training of structured generative models.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training is well-known, its application to theoretically constrained CSLC models for fine-tuning is unexplored. The novelty lies in carefully integrating spectral normalization and adversarial losses into a Gibbs sampling-based framework where the log-concavity condition must be preserved throughout the optimization.

proposals.title: Autoregressive Flows with Multi-Scale Attention for Coherent High-Dimensional Image Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Autoregressive models for high-dimensional image generation, often based on Gibbs distributions like PixelCNN, provide tractable likelihoods but struggle to capture long-range spatial dependencies, leading to incoherent global structure. Normalizing flows offer flexible density estimation but are typically applied to low-dimensional data or lack mechanisms for efficient global context integration in images. A synthesis is needed to combine the sequential modeling strength of autoregressive methods with the expressivity of flows and the global receptive field of attention.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a multi-scale attention mechanism into an autoregressive normalizing flow architecture will enable the explicit modeling of long-range spatial dependencies during sequential pixel generation, leading to improved global coherence and sample quality in high-dimensional image synthesis while maintaining exact likelihood computation.
proposals.method: We will develop a deep autoregressive flow model where each conditional distribution p(x_i | x_<i) is parameterized by a conditional normalizing flow (e.g., a stack of affine coupling layers). The conditioner for each flow will be a multi-scale attention network over the previously generated pixels, inspired by Transformer decoders and non-local neural networks. Specifically, we will implement a hierarchical attention mechanism that computes keys and values at multiple spatial resolutions from the context, allowing the model to adaptively focus on relevant regions (e.g., object boundaries, symmetries) at different scales during the sequential generation process. Training will proceed via maximum likelihood, with gradients computed through the flow transformations and attention layers.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a novel generative model architecture that unifies the exact likelihoods of autoregressive flows with the global contextual modeling of attention. It will provide a new tool for studying feature evolution and neural collapse in deep generative networks trained via maximum likelihood. The model will be benchmarked on standard image datasets (e.g., ImageNet, CelebA-HQ) against PixelCNN, Glow, and emerging diffusion models, with metrics for likelihood, sample quality (FID), and explicit tests of long-range coherence.
proposals.novelty: While attention has been used in autoregressive models (e.g., Image Transformer) and normalizing flows exist, their fusion for conditional flow-based prediction is unexplored. The key novelty is using multi-scale attention as the conditioning mechanism *within* each step of an autoregressive flow, directly guiding the flow's transformation based on a learned, global context.

proposals.title: Decoupling Conditioning from Invertibility: A Hybrid Variational Framework for Scalable Conditional Density Estimation
proposals.motivation: Conditional Normalizing Flows (CNFs) provide exact likelihood evaluation but are architecturally rigid, as the conditioning variable must be integrated into the invertible transformation, limiting the use of powerful, non-invertible feature extractors (e.g., transformers). This bottleneck is critical for high-dimensional data like images or graphs, where conditioning information is complex. A flexible framework that retains exact inference while leveraging state-of-the-art encoders is needed for scalable conditional modeling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that decoupling the conditioning mechanism from the flow's invertible layers via a variational latent bridge will yield more expressive and scalable conditional density estimators without sacrificing the tractable likelihood property of flows.
proposals.method: We propose a hybrid model where an arbitrary neural network encoder (e.g., a graph or vision transformer) processes the conditioning variable to output parameters of a Gaussian variational distribution in the latent space of a conditional normalizing flow. The flow, conditioned on samples from this variational distribution, models a complex base distribution. The system is trained end-to-end by maximizing the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO), which forces the marginal flow distribution to match the true conditional. We will analyze the gradient dynamics and feature evolution between the encoder and flow, drawing on tools from neural collapse and single index model analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, architecturally flexible framework for conditional density estimation that combines the strengths of variational inference and normalizing flows. It will enable the application of exact-likelihood models to domains with complex, high-dimensional conditioning variables, such as conditional image generation and graph-structured prediction tasks.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the variational decoupling of the conditioning pathway, which allows the use of unconstrained, non-invertible encoders within a flow-based model—a significant departure from standard CNF architectures that require conditioning within the invertible block.

proposals.title: Lagrangian Feature Flows: A Fluid Dynamics Analogy for Exact Conditional Density Estimation
proposals.motivation: Accurate density estimation for complex, high-dimensional data remains a core challenge in generative modeling. While energy-based models and GANs offer flexibility, they often lack tractable likelihoods or stable training dynamics. Drawing a formal analogy to the well-established Lagrangian framework in fluid dynamics, which elegantly tracks mass-conserving deformations, provides a principled foundation for constructing more stable and interpretable generative models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing normalizing flows explicitly as smooth transport maps within a Lagrangian analogy will yield theoretical insights into feature evolution and training dynamics, leading to more stable and efficient architectures for conditional density estimation.
proposals.method: We will construct a conditional normalizing flow where the invertible transformation is interpreted as a Lagrangian map, evolving particles (latent samples) from a simple base density. The model's architecture will be based on coupling layers (e.g., RealNVP) or continuous-time flows (Neural ODEs), with the Jacobian determinant explicitly linked to the volumetric deformation of the 'feature fluid.' Training will minimize the negative log-likelihood, derived via the change-of-variables formula, which directly corresponds to enforcing mass conservation in the fluid analogy. We will analyze the gradient flow of this objective to study feature evolution during training.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, physics-inspired theoretical framework for understanding normalizing flows, linking model properties (invertibility, Jacobian) to fluid mechanical principles. It will yield practical architectural guidelines for building stable flows and provide new tools for analyzing the dynamics of feature learning, connecting to phenomena like neural collapse.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic application of the Lagrangian fluid dynamics formalism to the architecture and analysis of normalizing flows, moving beyond a superficial metaphor to a mathematically grounded analogy that informs model design and theory.

proposals.title: Unified Image Restoration via Variational Data Assimilation in Diffusion Models
proposals.motivation: Current deep learning methods for joint super-resolution and denoising often treat the tasks separately or rely on deterministic mappings, lacking a coherent probabilistic framework. This limits their ability to quantify uncertainty and leverage the synergy between tasks. Drawing a formal analogy to data assimilation in geophysics, where noisy observations are fused with a physical prior, offers a principled path to a unified solution.
proposals.hypothesis: That a diffusion model, trained as a high-quality prior on clean, high-resolution images, can be integrated with a learned conditional likelihood model of the combined degradation process within a variational data assimilation framework. This will enable efficient sampling from the joint posterior, performing superior and probabilistically coherent restoration.
proposals.method: We will train a denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM) on clean, high-res images as the strong prior. A separate neural network will be trained to model the conditional score function of the likelihood, representing the combined downscaling and noise process. The core algorithm will be a novel variational inference procedure, inspired by 4D-Var data assimilation, that iteratively refines an initial state using gradients from both the prior and likelihood models to converge to a sample from the posterior distribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, unified probabilistic framework for multi-task image restoration, moving beyond sequential or deterministic approaches. It will yield a method that naturally quantifies uncertainty and offers a new perspective on integrating learned priors with forward models for inverse problems in vision.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the formal translation of variational data assimilation techniques from geophysics into a deep generative modeling context for vision. This differs from standard conditional diffusion by explicitly learning and leveraging a separate, structured likelihood model within a principled Bayesian inference loop.

proposals.title: Beyond Single-Unit Interpretability: A Statistical and Geometric Analysis of Feature Disentanglement in Deep Networks
proposals.motivation: The widespread assumption that individual neurons in high layers of deep networks correspond to human-interpretable concepts (e.g., 'cat detector') is a cornerstone of feature visualization and network dissection. However, this view is increasingly challenged by evidence of distributed representations and polysemantic neurons. A rigorous, formal challenge to this assumption is needed to guide more accurate interpretability methods.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in standard trained networks, human-interpretable, class-relevant features are not localized to individual high-level units but are instead distributed across subspaces defined by the network's final layer feature geometry, particularly under Neural Collapse conditions.
proposals.method: We will analyze feature evolution in trained CNNs and Graph Neural Networks, building on Bruna's recent work. Using tools from single-index models and conditional log-concavity, we will define a statistical measure of 'feature localization' versus 'subspace distribution'. We will then perform targeted interventions via gradient-based feature synthesis and ablation, not on single units, but on random directions within the feature manifold to test their causal role in class discrimination and human interpretability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, empirically grounded framework for moving beyond single-unit interpretability. It will contribute a new methodology for probing distributed features and offer theoretical insights linking the geometry of the final layer (Neural Collapse) to the structure of learned representations.
proposals.novelty: While distributed representations are known, our approach uniquely combines statistical modeling (single-index, log-concave) with a geometric, intervention-based analysis of feature subspaces to directly test the causal link between unit activation and interpretable concepts.

proposals.title: The Geometry of Instability: Characterizing and Inducing Discontinuous Input-Output Mappings in Trained Neural Networks
proposals.motivation: The prevailing assumption in deep learning theory and practice is that successful generalization is linked to learning smooth, stable functions. This motivates regularization techniques and architectural choices promoting Lipschitz continuity. However, this view may be incomplete, as certain tasks (e.g., classification with complex boundaries, generative modeling of multi-modal distributions) might inherently benefit from or even require controlled, learned discontinuities. Investigating this inverted assumption is crucial for a more complete theory of neural network function approximation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for specific, non-trivial function classes—particularly those arising in structured prediction and generative modeling—optimal or near-optimal neural networks will learn input-output mappings with essential, learned discontinuities that are structured and aligned with the data geometry, rather than being universally smooth.
proposals.method: We will analyze the layer-wise feature evolution and gradient structure of networks trained on synthetic and real-world tasks designed to necessitate discontinuous solutions (e.g., single-index models with sharp phase transitions, multi-class classification with adversarial robustness constraints).
