Drawing from Bruna's work on neural collapse and feature evolution, we will develop metrics to quantify the 'learned instability' of a mapping, such as the concentration of gradient norms near decision boundaries. We will then propose a novel training objective that explicitly penalizes unnecessary smoothness while promoting stability only along data manifolds, using conditional log-concave models as a testbed for controlled discontinuity in generative tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new theoretical and empirical framework for understanding when and how neural networks learn non-smooth functions. It will yield novel metrics for characterizing network mappings beyond simple Lipschitz bounds and could lead to new regularization schemes or architectures for tasks where smoothness is detrimental.
proposals.novelty: The proposal inverts the core smoothness dogma of generalization theory. Instead of viewing discontinuities as artifacts of poor training or overfitting, we formally investigate them as potentially learned, useful features of the network, connecting this perspective to geometric concepts like neural collapse and data-dependent feature stability.

proposals.title: Stabilizing Feature Evolution via Gradient-Guided Lipschitz Regularization
proposals.motivation: Deep neural networks are known to produce highly discontinuous input-output mappings, making them vulnerable to adversarial attacks and sensitive to small input perturbations. This lack of robustness and stability is a critical barrier to their reliable deployment in safety-critical applications. Understanding and controlling the evolution of features through network layers is a key step toward building more continuous and reliable models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly regularizing the gradient flow and the Lipschitz constants of intermediate feature mappings during training will lead to networks with more stable feature evolution, resulting in smoother, more robust input-output functions without sacrificing discriminative power.
proposals.method: We will develop a training procedure that penalizes the deviation of per-layer Jacobian singular values, encouraging approximate isometry in feature transformations. This builds on spectral normalization but applies it dynamically to the gradient of the layer's function with respect to its input. We will integrate this with a geometric analysis of feature evolution, drawing from Neural Collapse concepts, to ensure intra-class compactness and inter-class separability are maintained under this smoothness constraint. The training objective will combine standard cross-entropy with our novel gradient-stability regularizer.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel training framework for learning smoother network mappings, directly addressing adversarial vulnerability. It will contribute new theoretical insights into the relationship between feature evolution dynamics and output robustness. The resulting models are expected to show improved certified robustness and better generalization under distribution shifts.
proposals.novelty: While Lipschitz regularization exists, our method uniquely targets the stability of the *gradient* of the feature map itself, linking training dynamics (gradient flow) to the geometric properties of the learned representation. This integrates concepts from neural collapse and function space regularization in a novel, operationally feasible way.

proposals.title: Quantifying and Controlling Functional Discontinuity in Deep Neural Networks
proposals.motivation: Despite their empirical success, deep neural networks are known to be vulnerable to adversarial examples, which are often attributed to the networks' highly non-linear and potentially discontinuous nature. A rigorous, quantitative understanding of these discontinuities is lacking, limiting our ability to build robust and interpretable models. This project aims to formalize the concept of discontinuity in learned mappings, connecting it to training dynamics and generalization.
proposals.hypothesis: The functional discontinuity of a trained neural network can be systematically characterized via the geometry of its feature representations and the spectral properties of its layerwise Jacobians, and this measure correlates with adversarial vulnerability and generalization gaps.
proposals.method: We will develop a theoretical framework defining local and global discontinuity measures based on the modulus of continuity of the network function and the distribution of singular values of its input-output Jacobian. Building on recent work on Neural Collapse and feature evolution, we will analyze how training with gradient-based methods and weight decay shapes these discontinuities across layers. We will validate our theory empirically on image classification tasks (CIFAR, ImageNet) using standard architectures (ResNets, Vision Transformers), measuring the proposed discontinuity metrics and correlating them with adversarial robustness (via PGD attacks) and generalization error.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive framework for measuring functional discontinuity in neural networks, linking it to fundamental training phenomena. It will yield new diagnostic tools for model robustness and potentially inspire novel regularization techniques to control undesirable discontinuous behavior.
proposals.novelty: While prior work studies Lipschitz constants or Jacobian norms, we propose a multi-scale, feature-centric analysis of discontinuity that is explicitly tied to the training process and the recently observed Neural Collapse phenomenon, offering a more nuanced and actionable characterization.

proposals.title: Adversarial Feature Evolution and Neural Collapse in Non-Visual Modalities
proposals.motivation: Adversarial robustness analysis has been predominantly focused on image classification networks, providing deep insights into feature geometry and decision boundaries. However, the fundamental principles governing how adversarial perturbations affect feature evolution and training dynamics in networks for speech, text, or graph data remain poorly understood. Extending this analysis is crucial for building robust, trustworthy AI systems across all data domains.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the adversarial training dynamics for non-visual tasks exhibit a form of structured feature evolution, potentially leading to a variant of the Neural Collapse phenomenon, where within-class features collapse and between-class feature separability is maximized under adversarial constraints.
proposals.method: We will apply adversarial perturbation methods (e.g., Projected Gradient Descent) to pre-trained and newly trained networks for speech recognition (e.g., Wav2Vec 2.0) and text classification (e.g., BERT). We will analyze the evolution of intermediate layer features during adversarial training, measuring metrics of within-class and between-class variability. The analysis will be grounded in the theoretical framework of conditionally log-concave models and single-index models to characterize the feature geometry and gradient behavior in these high-dimensional, structured input spaces.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic study of adversarial feature evolution in speech and language models, linking empirical observations to theoretical concepts like Neural Collapse. It will yield new benchmarks for adversarial robustness in these modalities and inform the design of more robust training objectives for non-visual data.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial attacks are known for these modalities, a rigorous, feature-dynamics-focused analysis connecting adversarial training to geometric phenomena like Neural Collapse is novel. The application of Joan Bruna's recent theoretical work on feature evolution and generative models to adversarial robustness in non-Euclidean data spaces is a significant departure from existing literature.

proposals.title: Manifold Structure and Neural Collapse in High-Level Activation Spaces
proposals.motivation: Understanding the geometry of learned representations is crucial for interpreting and improving deep neural networks. Pre-2026 work, including the Neural Collapse phenomenon, has shown that class features often collapse to a simplex structure in the final layer, but the evolution and intrinsic geometry of high-level unit activations in intermediate layers remain less understood. Characterizing this semantic structure via manifold learning can bridge the gap between feature learning theory and practical model behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the high-level unit activation vectors for a given class lie on or near a low-dimensional, smooth manifold, and that the training dynamics (gradient flow) and architectural constraints (e.g., graph structure in GNNs) govern the geometric properties of these class-specific manifolds and their relative arrangements.
proposals.method: We will analyze the activation space of penultimate layers in trained vision and graph neural networks. Using pre-2026 manifold learning techniques (e.g., UMAP, Riemannian metric learning, and diffusion maps), we will empirically model the intrinsic geometry of activations per class. We will then link these geometric properties to training dynamics by studying the gradient-induced feature evolution and to architectural factors by extending the analysis to Graph Neural Networks, connecting to the neural collapse perspective on feature evolution in non-Euclidean domains.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel geometric framework for analyzing representation learning, moving beyond mean-vector analysis to describe the full manifold structure of classes. It will yield new metrics for model interpretability and robustness, and offer insights into how network architecture influences the emergent geometry of semantic representations.
proposals.novelty: While manifold learning has been applied to raw data, its systematic application to model the semantic, class-conditional structure of *internal unit activations* is novel. Furthermore, integrating this with the neural collapse framework and extending it to non-Euclidean data structures (GNNs) provides a unified geometric perspective on feature evolution.

proposals.title: Inverse Feature Synthesis: Recovering Robust Inputs from Activation Space Semantic Directions
proposals.motivation: Modern deep networks learn rich, hierarchical feature representations, but understanding the precise input patterns that align with semantic concepts remains a core challenge. This inverse problem is crucial for interpretability, robustness verification, and improving training dynamics, as seen in phenomena like Neural Collapse where features within a class collapse to a single direction. Pre-2026, methods for feature visualization or activation maximization often produce unrealistic or adversarial inputs, failing to recover robust, semantically meaningful patterns.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating the recovery of inputs from unit activation space as a constrained inverse problem within a conditionally strongly log-concave generative model, we can synthesize robust, naturalistic inputs that are maximally aligned with semantic feature directions, moving beyond adversarial artifacts.
proposals.method: We will leverage the geometric structure of the unit activation space in trained neural networks, building on the Neural Collapse perspective to define stable class and attribute directions. The core algorithm will involve solving an inverse optimization: given a target semantic direction in a chosen layer, we will search the input space to maximize alignment, regularized by a pre-trained generative prior (e.g., a diffusion model or normalizing flow) conditioned on the feature direction. This conditioning will be informed by analysis of Single Index Models to link feature directions to structured input variations. The optimization will use projected gradient methods to remain within the data manifold, ensuring robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method for synthesizing semantically-aligned, robust inputs, directly contributing to feature interpretability. It will establish a theoretical and practical bridge between the geometry of activation spaces (Neural Collapse) and the structure of the input manifold. The resulting techniques can be used to audit feature robustness and generate targeted data for training.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its integration of conditionally generative models as priors for solving the activation-space inverse problem, explicitly targeting robust feature recovery rather than mere activation maximization. It uniquely combines insights from Neural Collapse geometry, Single Index Model analysis, and log-concave generative modeling for this task.

proposals.title: Implicit Relational Learning via Gradient-Guided Metric Attention
proposals.motivation: Many graph-based learning methods rely on a pre-specified or explicitly estimated graph structure, which is often noisy, computationally expensive to obtain, and may not align with the downstream task. This bottleneck limits the applicability of relational reasoning to domains where explicit relations are unknown or dynamic. Implicitly learning the relational structure directly from data could lead to more flexible and powerful models.
proposals.hypothesis: That a neural network can implicitly learn task-optimal relational structures through a combination of gradient-based feature alignment and adaptive attention mechanisms, without requiring an explicit graph estimation step, and that this process exhibits a stable collapse phenomenon akin to Neural Collapse in graph-structured feature evolution.
proposals.method: We will design a neural architecture where node or data point features interact through a multi-head attention mechanism whose key/query projections are parameterized by a learnable, input-dependent metric. This metric will be regularized via a gradient alignment objective, encouraging features from the same class to evolve toward a simplex Equiangular Tight Frame (ETF) structure, as observed in Neural Collapse, thereby implicitly defining relational strength. We will analyze the dynamics of this system, drawing from recent work on feature evolution in GNNs and single-index models, to theoretically characterize the emergent implicit graph.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new framework for relational learning that bypasses explicit graph construction, theoretically linking implicit structure learning to feature collapse phenomena. It will yield practical algorithms for relational tasks in non-graph data and offer new insights into how attention mechanisms self-organize representations.
proposals.novelty: While attention is used for relational reasoning, coupling it with a gradient-guided metric learning objective to *induce* Neural Collapse dynamics for implicit graph formation is novel. This moves beyond using attention on a given graph to using it as a mechanism for discovering the graph itself, grounded in feature dynamics theory.

proposals.title: Sample Complexity and Generalization Bounds for Spectral Graph Neural Networks on Noisy Graph Estimations
proposals.motivation: Spectral Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are widely used for learning on graph-structured data, but their theoretical guarantees typically assume a perfectly known graph. In practice, graphs are often estimated from noisy data (e.g., in neuroscience or social networks), introducing a mismatch between the assumed and true data geometry. Pre-2026, there is a lack of formal analysis quantifying how this estimation error propagates through spectral network training, affecting sample complexity and generalization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the sample complexity of Spectral Networks trained on estimated graphs can be formally bounded by a function of the spectral norm of the graph estimation error, and that this leads to a quantifiable trade-off between graph estimation quality and required training data.
proposals.method: We will analyze a class of Spectral Networks defined via learnable spectral filters. Using tools from statistical learning theory and matrix perturbation analysis, we will derive Rademacher complexity and uniform stability bounds. The core technical step involves decomposing the population risk to isolate the contribution of the graph estimation error matrix. We will specifically study the gradient dynamics of the feature evolution across layers under this perturbation, linking it to the neural collapse phenomena observed in simpler settings.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous learning complexity guarantees for Spectral Networks operating on estimated graphs, offering practitioners a principled framework for allocating resources between graph refinement and model training. It will extend the theory of feature evolution and neural collapse to the non-Euclidean, perturbed-graph setting.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes GNN stability or spectral approximation, a direct characterization of sample complexity that explicitly incorporates the statistical error of graph estimation is novel. Bridging the analysis of gradient-based feature learning with matrix perturbation theory for spectral filters represents a significant theoretical advance.

proposals.title: A Comparative Analysis of Feature Evolution: Benchmarking Dropout Networks against Non-Euclidean Deep Learning Baselines
proposals.motivation: Recent theoretical work, such as the Neural Collapse (NC) phenomenon, provides a powerful lens to study feature evolution in deep networks. However, empirical studies of NC and related training dynamics are often limited to standard Euclidean architectures. A systematic comparison against established non-Euclidean approaches is needed to understand if the observed feature properties are universal or architecture-dependent.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the geometric properties of feature evolution, including the emergence of Neural Collapse, will differ qualitatively and quantitatively between Dropout Networks and other non-Euclidean deep learning methods, revealing a dependence on the underlying data geometry and optimization constraints.
proposals.method: We will extend the analysis framework from Bruna's work on feature evolution in Graph Neural Networks to a controlled empirical study. We will train models on standard image classification benchmarks (CIFAR-10/100, ImageNet) and compare Dropout Networks against specific non-Euclidean baselines: 1) Hyperbolic Neural Networks (using the Poincaré ball model), 2) SPDNet (neural networks on the manifold of Symmetric Positive Definite matrices), and 3) Geodesic Convolutional Neural Networks for manifold-valued data. We will track within-class and between-class feature variability, gradient flow, and final layer geometry throughout training.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive benchmark relating training dynamics in Dropout Networks to other geometric deep learning paradigms. It will clarify the scope of Neural Collapse theory and offer practical guidance on selecting architectures based on desired feature properties. The resulting code and metrics will serve as a new evaluation suite for non-Euclidean deep learning.
proposals.novelty: While NC has been studied in CNNs and Transformers, a direct comparison to manifold-based networks is novel. The proposal uniquely bridges Bruna's work on feature evolution with the geometric deep learning community's focus on inductive biases, moving beyond simple performance metrics to analyze intrinsic training dynamics.

proposals.title: Manifold Learning for Graph Inference: A Neural Collapse and Gradient Flow Perspective
proposals.motivation: Inferring latent graph structures from high-dimensional data is fundamental in neuroscience, social networks, and single-cell biology. However, classical methods often rely on simple similarity metrics, ignoring the complex, low-dimensional geometry of the data manifold. This disconnect limits the accuracy and interpretability of the estimated graphs, especially for modern, non-Euclidean datasets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the geometric structure of the data manifold, as revealed through the feature evolution and neural collapse dynamics of trained neural networks, provides a superior inductive bias for graph inference compared to naive distance-based methods.
proposals.method: We will train a Graph Neural Network (GNN) on the high-dimensional data for a pretext task (e.g., node classification or reconstruction). We will then analyze the evolution of node features across layers and training time, using tools from neural collapse to characterize the geometry of the final feature embedding. The inferred graph will be constructed by modeling the pairwise relationships between data points based on their trajectories in this learned feature space, using the gradient flow of the network's training objective to inform the strength and direction of edges.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, theoretically grounded framework for graph estimation that is intrinsically linked to the data's underlying manifold. It will extend the theory of neural collapse from classification to unsupervised representation learning and offer a new interpretation of GNN training dynamics as a process of latent graph discovery.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly connecting the geometric principles of manifold learning and neural collapse—phenomena observed in supervised classification—to the unsupervised problem of graph inference, using the training dynamics and feature evolution of the network itself as the primary signal for graph construction.

proposals.title: Temporal Graph Neural Networks via Recurrent Feature Evolution: A Neural Collapse Analysis
proposals.motivation: Many real-world systems, such as social networks, biological interactions, and sensor grids, generate sequential data where the underlying relational structure is dynamic and crucial for prediction. Pre-2026 methods often treat graph structure and node features separately across time, failing to capture their joint, co-evolutionary dynamics in a unified, theoretically grounded framework. This limits performance in tasks like dynamic node classification and sequential graph-level prediction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating recurrent neural networks (RNNs) with graph convolutional layers to model the sequential evolution of node features will lead to a structured, low-dimensional feature evolution that exhibits properties analogous to Neural Collapse, thereby improving generalization and interpretability for sequence-graph tasks.
proposals.method: We propose a Recurrent Graph Convolutional Network (RGCN) architecture where the hidden state of a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) cell at each node is updated by a graph convolution over its neighbors' previous hidden states and external inputs. We will analyze the gradient flow and feature evolution dynamics across layers and time steps, drawing on tools from our prior work on feature evolution in GNNs and single-index models. Training will be studied under conditional log-concave assumptions on the data distribution to understand the implicit regularization of the recurrent graph convolution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, unified architecture for sequence-graph modeling with a theoretical analysis of its feature learning dynamics. It will extend the Neural Collapse phenomenon to temporal graph domains, offering new insights into the training and generalization of recurrent graph networks. The resulting framework will be validated on dynamic node classification and temporal link prediction benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the principled integration of RNNs with graph convolutions specifically to study the *feature evolution* across time, analyzed through the lens of Neural Collapse—a perspective not yet applied to temporal GNNs. This moves beyond simple architectural combination to a theoretical study of training dynamics in sequence-graphs.

proposals.title: Graph Neural Collapse: Learning Compressed Graph Representations via Neural Feature Evolution
proposals.motivation: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are primarily used for node or graph-level prediction, treating the graph structure as a fixed input. However, many real-world graphs are large, noisy, and dynamically evolving, necessitating methods to learn a compressed, task-relevant representation of the graph itself. Pre-2026, there is limited work on using the internal feature evolution of a GNN to directly distill the graph's structural essence, a dual problem to standard supervised learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the neural collapse phenomenon—where deep network features converge to a simplex Equiangular Tight Frame (ETF) geometry during terminal training—can be leveraged to learn a maximally separable and compressed representation of a graph's structural information, independent of specific node labels.
proposals.method: We will train a GNN encoder with a reconstruction objective on the graph's adjacency or diffusion matrix, using a bottleneck layer to enforce compression. The core innovation is to analyze and guide the feature evolution in the penultimate layer, applying regularization derived from the neural collapse theory to encourage the learned graph-level features to converge to a stable, class-conditional ETF structure, where each 'class' corresponds to a distinct graph motif or community. Training will involve a joint loss combining reconstruction error, a collapse-promoting term (e.g., based on within-class and between-class feature covariance), and gradient alignment analysis to study the dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for unsupervised graph compression grounded in the theory of neural collapse. It will provide new insights into how GNNs internally represent structural information and deliver a practical method for generating robust, low-dimensional graph embeddings suitable for downstream tasks like retrieval, clustering, and anomaly detection.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in reframing graph compression as a neural collapse process, explicitly using the geometric properties of terminal training dynamics—a concept studied for classification—to learn structural representations, rather than applying generic autoencoder or matrix factorization techniques.

proposals.title: Low-Rank Compression of Fully Connected Layers via Gradient-Based Feature Evolution Analysis
proposals.motivation: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are widely deployed, but their large, dense fully connected (FC) layers create significant storage and computational bottlenecks, especially for edge devices. While low-rank approximations are well-studied for convolutional layers, principled compression of FC layers remains less explored, often relying on simple truncation of pre-trained weights. A theory-driven approach that accounts for the feature evolution and training dynamics of these layers is needed for efficient compression without catastrophic performance loss.
proposals.hypothesis: The feature evolution dynamics in FC layers, particularly under a neural collapse regime, exhibit low-rank structure that can be identified and exploited during training to learn compressed, factorized representations directly, outperforming post-hoc decomposition methods.
proposals.method: We will extend the gradient-based feature analysis from Bruna's work on Graph Neural Networks to FC layers in standard CNNs (e.g., ResNets). The method involves: 1) Analyzing the gradient flow and feature covariance matrices of FC layers during training to identify stable low-rank subspaces. 2) Formulating a constrained optimization where the FC layer is parameterized as the product of two low-rank matrices, with the rank determined adaptively via spectral analysis of the evolving features. 3) Integrating this structured parameterization into the training loop, regularized to maintain alignment with the gradient dynamics observed in the uncompressed network.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, training-aware framework for compressing FC layers, yielding models with significantly fewer parameters and faster inference. It will contribute a theoretical understanding of feature evolution in FC layers under compression, bridging the gap between neural collapse theory and practical model efficiency.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard post-training matrix factorization, this approach jointly learns the low-rank factorization and the network weights based on training dynamics. It uniquely applies principles of feature evolution and gradient analysis—typically studied for theoretical insight—to the practical problem of architectural compression.

proposals.title: Inducing Low-Rank Convolutional Filters via Spectral Regularization and Gradient-Based Training Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Modern convolutional neural networks (CNNs) are often over-parameterized, leading to high computational cost and potential overfitting. Prior to 2026, post-hoc low-rank approximations of filters are used for compression, but they degrade performance. Encouraging filters to be inherently low-rank during training could yield more efficient and generalizable architectures from the outset, aligning with the broader goal of understanding and simplifying feature learning.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly penalizing the nuclear norm of convolutional filter tensors, combined with an analysis of the induced gradient dynamics on the singular value spectrum, will lead to trained networks whose filters possess inherently low-rank structure without significant loss of discriminative power.
proposals.method: We will formulate a novel training objective adding a weighted spectral (nuclear norm) regularizer to the standard cross-entropy loss, applied to the 4D tensor of each convolutional layer. We will analyze the gradient flow of this objective with respect to the singular values of the filters, drawing on tools from the neural collapse and feature evolution literature. The training procedure will be implemented and tested on standard image classification benchmarks (CIFAR, ImageNet) using ResNet and VGG-style architectures, with ablation studies on regularization schedules.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled training method to learn compact convolutional representations directly, reducing the need for post-hoc compression. It will contribute a theoretical analysis of how regularization shapes the spectral evolution of filters, connecting practical optimization to geometric properties of the feature space.
proposals.novelty: While low-rank regularization is known in matrix completion, its systematic integration and dynamical analysis for inducing low-rank structure *during* CNN training, rather than after, is unexplored. The approach moves beyond simple weight decay or post-hoc decomposition by targeting the singular value distribution explicitly via the training gradient.

proposals.title: Compressing Massive Convolutional Ensembles via Neural Collapse and Gradient-Based Feature Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Massive ensembles of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) provide state-of-the-art robustness and accuracy but are computationally prohibitive for deployment. Pre-2026, methods for compressing single models are mature, but principled approaches for compressing large ensembles—where diversity is key—remain underexplored. This work addresses the critical need for efficient, high-performance ensemble deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: The neural collapse phenomenon, observed in the terminal phase of training for single classifiers, provides a structured, low-dimensional geometric template that can be leveraged to synthesize a compact set of features capable of approximating the predictive diversity of a massive convolutional ensemble.
proposals.method: We will train a large, diverse ensemble of CNNs on image classification tasks. Inspired by Bruna's work on feature evolution and neural collapse, we will analyze the geometry of final-layer features across the ensemble. We will then train a single, compact student model using a novel distillation loss that matches not only output probabilities but also the gradient-based feature trajectories (relating to Bruna's 'gradient' and 'functions' themes) that encode ensemble diversity. The compression will explicitly enforce a conditionally strong log-concave structure on the synthesized feature space to ensure stable training.
proposals.contribution: This will yield a novel, theoretically grounded framework for extreme ensemble compression, directly linking the geometry of neural collapse to practical model distillation. It will provide empirical benchmarks for compressing massive convolutional ensembles with minimal performance loss, advancing efficient deep learning deployment.
proposals.novelty: While neural collapse has been studied in single models, its application for distilling ensemble diversity via gradient-aligned feature synthesis is novel. The approach uniquely combines concepts from neural collapse geometry, gradient-based feature matching, and generative log-concave models for compression.

proposals.title: Stability and Feature Evolution in Advanced Graph Neural Networks: A Theoretical Analysis of Attention and Jump Knowledge Architectures
proposals.motivation: Stability analysis, which studies how small perturbations in graph structure affect node representations, provides a crucial theoretical framework for understanding the generalization and robustness of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). Prior work, including foundational contributions by Joan Bruna and colleagues, has established stability bounds primarily for simple spectral and spatial convolutional architectures. However, the widespread adoption of more complex, state-of-the-art architectures like Graph Attention Networks (GATs) and Jumping Knowledge Networks (JK-Nets) necessitates extending this analysis to ensure their theoretical grounding and guide principled design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the stability properties proven for simple GCN layers do not universally hold for attention-based or multi-scale architectures, and that their feature evolution dynamics can be characterized by modified stability bounds dependent on architectural components like attention mechanisms and adaptive depth.
proposals.method: We will extend the existing stability analysis framework, which leverages tools from graph signal processing and concentration of measure, to two key architectures: Graph Attention Networks (GAT) and Jumping Knowledge Networks (JK-Nets). For GATs, we will analyze how the learned attention weights and their gradient dynamics during training affect the Lipschitz constant of the layer. For JK-Nets, we will study the stability implications of combining features from different depths via concatenation or max-pooling. The analysis will involve deriving perturbation bounds for node features as a function of graph edge perturbations, explicitly tracking the role of attention coefficients and skip connections.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first theoretical stability guarantees for prominent advanced GNN architectures, bridging a gap between empirical success and theoretical understanding. It will yield practical insights into the robustness trade-offs of using attention versus fixed filters and of combining multi-scale features, informing architecture selection for stability-sensitive applications.
proposals.novelty: While stability is well-studied for basic GNNs, a systematic analysis of how adaptive mechanisms (attention) and feature aggregation schemes (jump knowledge) alter stability is novel and non-trivial, as these components introduce data-dependent, non-linear operations that break the assumptions of prior analyses.

proposals.title: The Adversarial Geometry of Graph Neural Networks: Characterizing Maximally Destabilizing Perturbations via a Stability Duality
proposals.motivation: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are increasingly deployed in high-stakes domains like social network analysis and molecular discovery, where robustness to small structural changes is critical. While stability analysis, often via Lipschitz constants, provides worst-case bounds on output changes, it fails to identify the specific, minimal graph perturbations that most severely degrade performance. Characterizing these 'maximally destabilizing' perturbations is essential for diagnosing model vulnerabilities and improving certified robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a stable GNN, the perturbations causing the largest output change relative to their size are not random but lie along specific, data-dependent directions in graph space, which can be found by solving a dual optimization problem related to the network's stability certificate.
proposals.method: We will formulate a constrained optimization problem: given a trained GNN and input graph, find the minimal graph edit (in edge additions/deletions) that maximizes the change in the node- or graph-level output. We will derive a Lagrangian dual to this primal problem, linking the search for destabilizing perturbations to the gradient of the loss with respect to the adjacency matrix, regularized by the stability constraint (e.g., a spectral norm bound). This will be implemented via projected gradient ascent on the perturbation space, with efficient approximations for the constraint projection using power iteration, tailored to architectures like GCNs and GATs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel theoretical and algorithmic framework for adversarial graph generation focused on instability rather than mere misclassification. It will yield interpretable 'fingerprints' of GNN vulnerability—the most sensitive graph substructures—and offer a new perspective on the feature evolution dynamics in GNNs under adversarial stress, connecting to the neural collapse phenomenon in non-Euclidean domains.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard adversarial attacks that seek any misclassification, we explicitly target the instability boundary of provably stable models, creating a duality between stability certificates and worst-case perturbations. This connects the geometric analysis of feature evolution (a core Bruna theme) directly to adversarial robustness in graphs.

ideas.title: Implicit Feature Extraction via Neural Network Pruning for Multi-Modal Distributions
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that explicit sufficient statistics are necessary to capture multi-modality by drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience. In sensory processing, the brain uses sparse, pruned neural networks to represent complex stimuli without pre-defined summary statistics. Adapting this, we propose training a neural network with sparsity constraints directly on data from a multi-modal distribution. The learning process will prune the network to retain only the pathways essential for discriminating between modes, resulting in an architecture whose connectivity and activations implicitly encode the required statistical features, bypassing the need for their explicit definition.

ideas.title: Hybrid Variational Framework with Unconstrained Conditioning for Flexible Conditional Density Estimation
ideas.description: This system overcomes the architectural rigidity of standard conditional normalizing flows by decoupling the conditioning mechanism from the invertible flow. A powerful, arbitrary neural network encoder processes the conditioning variable and outputs parameters for a variational distribution in the flow's latent space. A conditional normalizing flow then models a complex base distribution, and the whole system is trained by maximizing a variational lower bound that ensures the flow's output distribution matches the true conditional distribution. This approach retains the exact likelihood evaluation of flows while enabling the use of state-of-the-art, non-invertible architectures (e.g., transformers, attention modules) for processing complex conditioning information, leading to more scalable and expressive models for high-dimensional data.

ideas.title: Implicit Sufficient Statistics via Neural Surrogates for Stable, Efficient Training
ideas.description: This research direction proposes replacing the explicit calculation of complex sufficient statistics (e.g., for multi-modal data) with a small, concurrently trained convolutional or transformer neural surrogate. This surrogate acts as a differentiable estimator, implicitly capturing the necessary statistical features. By integrating this surrogate's outputs directly into the core training algorithm's update rules—effectively serving as an adaptive, context-aware preconditioner—the method reduces per-iteration cost and memory overhead. This co-adaptation between the statistic estimator and the main model parameters leads to a more stable and efficient optimization landscape, particularly for distributions where traditional explicit statistics are expensive or ill-defined.

ideas.title: Lightweight Graph Neural Networks for Real-Time Substructure-Aware Image Super-Resolution
ideas.description: This research develops a lightweight Graph Neural Network (GNN) for mobile super-resolution that fundamentally relaxes the discrete attribute assumptions of traditional graph-based image processing. It models an image's local patch structure as a graph where nodes and edges carry rich, continuous feature vectors (e.g., gradient, texture descriptors) and employs highly optimized, approximate algorithms for counting or weighting small, meaningful graph substructures (like specific edge patterns or small cycles) to guide the upscaling. The core innovation is a co-design of the substructure-feature representation and the neural architecture, enabling the model to capture nuanced, non-local relationships critical for perceptual quality while maintaining the extreme efficiency required for real-time inference on resource-constrained hardware. This results in a super-resolution system that achieves a new trade-off: the detail preservation of structure-aware methods with the speed and portability of lightweight mobile models.

ideas.title: Variance-Adaptive Warm-up: A Plug-in Method for Stabilizing and Accelerating Optimizer Convergence
ideas.description: This work identifies heuristic warm-up schedules as a key source of early-training instability and wasted computation in modern deep learning. We introduce a variance-adaptive warm-up (VAW) module that continuously estimates the gradient variance during the initial training phase and uses it to control the optimizer's effective learning rate, ensuring it remains within a stable, high-progress regime. This approach provides a provable bound on early-step loss changes and serves as a plug-in replacement for existing warm-up heuristics in frameworks like PyTorch and TensorFlow. Empirical validation across vision and language tasks demonstrates faster convergence, reduced sensitivity to the initial learning rate hyperparameter, and improved final performance compared to standard schedules.

ideas.title: Joint Image Restoration via a Multi-Physics Informed Diffusion Process
ideas.description: This idea scales the joint modeling of conditional distributions for super-resolution and denoising by drawing an analogy from atmospheric data assimilation. In that field, noisy, incomplete sensor data is fused with a physics-based prior model to produce a complete, accurate state estimate. Adapted to computer vision, we treat the dual-task problem as an inverse problem: given a low-resolution, noisy observation, we use a powerful prior (a diffusion model trained on clean, high-resolution images) and a learned model of the conditional likelihood (encoding the combined downscaling and noise process). The solution is a scalable inference procedure, analogous to variational or sequential data assimilation, that samples from the joint posterior distribution, thereby performing super-resolution and denoising in a single, unified, and probabilistically coherent framework.

ideas.title: Meta-Adversarial Training for Degradation-Agnostic Gibbs Model Adaptation
ideas.description: This approach integrates meta-learning and adversarial training into a unified framework for the Gibbs model. The system meta-learns an initial generator (the Gibbs model) and a discriminator across a diverse task distribution of degradations. The adversarial training loop is then embedded within the meta-learning process, forcing the generator to learn internal representations and update rules that are effective not just for reconstructing data, but for strategically countering the discriminative capabilities of a co-evolving adversary across tasks. This results in a model that can rapidly adapt to a novel corruption by leveraging its experience in adversarially constructing robust priors, achieving a level of generalization and corruption-invariance that neither technique alone provides.

ideas.title: Implicit Sufficiency via Architectural Inductive Biases in Multi-Modal Fusion
ideas.description: This research investigates novel CNN (or hybrid CNN/Transformer) architectures whose structural inductive biases are designed to inherently satisfy the properties of sufficiency for multi-modal data fusion, without ever explicitly computing a sufficient statistic. The core hypothesis is that specific connectivity patterns, regularization schemes, and bottleneck designs can compel the network to learn a representation that is, by construction, information-preserving with respect to the task-relevant signal. This approach moves beyond simply searching for better explicit statistics or abandoning statistical principles altogether, instead using architecture as a mechanism to enforce an implicit sufficiency guarantee, leading to more robust and interpretable multi-modal models.

ideas.title: Adapting Evolutionary Biology's Neutral Theory to CNN Filter Initialization
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that CNN filters require multiscale complex wavelet initialization for effective learning. By drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology's Neutral Theory—which successfully challenged the assumption that all genetic variation must be adaptive—we propose a parallel 'Neutral Initialization' hypothesis for CNNs. The analogous solution was to establish a null model where random, non-adaptive genetic drift explains most variation, against which adaptive selection could be tested. Adapted to deep learning, this means treating wavelet initialization as a strong, potentially over-specified prior and testing whether simpler, random or minimally structured initializations (the 'neutral' model) perform statistically indistinguishably or even better after training. The research would involve rigorous comparative experiments across datasets and architectures, measuring not just final accuracy but also convergence dynamics and the diversity of learned feature manifolds, to determine if the presumed necessity of wavelet structure is a valid adaptive prior or an unnecessary constraint that the optimization process can overcome through neutral weight drift.

ideas.title: Apply normalizing flows for density estimation using a transport map analogy from fluid dynamics
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from fluid dynamics, where smooth transport maps model the movement and density evolution of fluid parcels. The analogous solution uses a Lagrangian framework to track parcels via an invertible map, computing final density through the map's deformation (Jacobian). Adapted to machine learning, we propose using normalizing flows to act as this learned transport map. The flow transforms samples from a simple base distribution through a series of invertible, differentiable transformations to model a complex conditional distribution directly. This bypasses the need for a Gibbs energy formulation, enabling exact likelihood evaluation and efficient sampling by leveraging the flow's invertibility and tractable Jacobian determinant, much like computing density changes in a fluid flow.

ideas.title: Attention-Guided Autoregressive Flow Models for High-Dimensional Image Generation
ideas.description: This research direction proposes replacing the Gibbs distribution framework with an autoregressive flow model, such as a normalizing flow, for the conditional pixel prediction task. Crucially, a multi-scale attention mechanism, inspired by Transformer architectures, is integrated to allow the flow model to learn long-range spatial dependencies and adaptively focus on relevant image regions during the sequential generation process. This synergy enables the model to capture complex, global image structure in a tractable probabilistic manner that Gibbs-based models struggle with, while maintaining the density estimation benefits of flow models. The result is a novel generative architecture capable of high-dimensional image synthesis with explicit likelihoods and improved global coherence.

The system is trained on a diverse meta-dataset of tasks and architectures to predict an initialization distribution that maximizes early training convergence and final performance for a novel, unseen task. The resulting conditioned initialization policy can blend wavelet structure, randomness, and other inductive biases, providing a robust, adaptive solution superior to any single fixed method.

ideas.title: Curriculum-Cascaded Super-Resolution for Extreme Information Recovery
ideas.description: This research proposes a cascaded conditional generative model that learns extreme super-resolution through a curriculum of progressively harder scaling tasks. Instead of a single large jump, the system is trained on a sequence of scaling ratios (e.g., 4x, then 8x, then 16x, up to 128x), where each stage is a specialized network conditioned on all previous outputs. This architecture explicitly manages the information gap, using adversarial and feature-matching losses at each stage to enforce realism and fidelity. The final system can reliably generate plausible, high-detail images from extremely low-resolution inputs by constructing the solution through a series of verified, intermediate representations.

ideas.title: Meta-Learning the Conditional Observation Process for Universal Inverse Problem Adaptation
ideas.description: This approach meta-learns a flexible, parameterized model of the conditional distribution of low-resolution observations given a high-resolution signal. Instead of learning a single inverse mapping, the model learns to quickly adapt its understanding of the forward degradation process from a few examples of a new corruption type. This adapted forward model is then analytically or algorithmically inverted to perform high-quality super-resolution for the novel degradation. The synergy enables a single system to solve a wide range of inverse problems by learning to characterize the corruption itself, moving beyond adaptation of just the restoration function.

ideas.title: Thumbnail Generation via Cartographic Generalization Principles
ideas.description: This idea applies the principles of cartographic generalization—the process of reducing detail in a map when scaling down—to the problem of generating effective thumbnails from high-resolution images. In cartography, solutions involve rule-based algorithms for selection, simplification, and exaggeration to preserve critical information. Adapted to computer vision, this involves designing a neural network trained with a loss function that encodes these generalization principles, guiding it to produce thumbnails that are not just pixel-subsamples but intelligently simplified representations that maintain recognizability, composition, and key visual elements of the original image.

ideas.title: Temporal Graph Neural Networks for Dynamic Audio Super-Resolution
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel audio super-resolution method that models the time-frequency representation of an audio signal as a dynamic graph, where nodes represent spectral components and edges capture their temporal and harmonic relationships. A temporal graph neural network is then applied to this evolving structure, using dynamic substructure patterns to predict and reconstruct high-resolution audio. This approach allows the model to explicitly reason about how spectral features like transients, pitch, and formants change over time, a capability missing from standard convolutional or static graph-based methods. The result is a super-resolution system that achieves higher fidelity by preserving the temporal coherence and natural evolution of audio events.

ideas.title: Adapting Mean-Field Theory from Statistical Physics for Structured Variational Likelihood Approximation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core principle of mean-field theory from statistical physics to the context of variational inference for likelihood approximation. In statistical physics, mean-field theory solves the intractable problem of many interacting bodies by approximating the influence of the system on a single body as an average static field. Analogously, we propose a mean-field variational inference (MFVI) method where the complex, true posterior distribution over latent variables is approximated by a fully factorized variational distribution (e.g., a product of independent Gaussians). This structural constraint simplifies the optimization of the evidence lower bound (ELBO), making the approximation of the conditional log-likelihood computationally efficient and scalable for high-dimensional models, much like mean-field theory enables the study of complex macroscopic phenomena from simple microscopic rules.

ideas.title: Combinatorial Enumeration for Inverse Problems in Structured Gibbs Distributions
ideas.description: This research direction fuses combinatorial enumeration techniques with the Gibbs distribution framework for inverse problems. It proposes to tackle high-dimensional structured prediction problems, such as inferring graph distributions or combinatorial objects from aggregate data, by exploiting the discrete, combinatorial nature of the state space. Instead of relying on approximate super-resolution or variational methods, the core idea is to develop exact or parameterized enumeration algorithms to compute the partition function and marginals of the relevant Gibbs distribution. This synergy enables principled, scalable solutions to inverse problems where the structure is inherently combinatorial, moving beyond continuous approximations to leverage discrete mathematical foundations directly.

ideas.title: Blind Seismic Deconvolution with Hierarchical Bayesian Uncertainty
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to the analogous inverse problem in geophysics—blind seismic deconvolution—to the domain of computer vision. In seismic imaging, hierarchical Bayesian models with MCMC sampling are used to jointly infer an unknown seismic wavelet (blur kernel) and the Earth's reflectivity (sharp image) while rigorously quantifying uncertainty. We propose applying this conditional Gibbs (MCMC) framework to blind image deblurring. The model will place hierarchical priors on the sharp image's gradients (e.g., a sparse heavy-tailed distribution) and the blur kernel (e.g., enforcing smoothness and support constraints). By sampling from the joint posterior of the image, kernel, and model hyperparameters, the method will provide a probabilistic characterization of the solution, delivering not just a single deblurred estimate but a distribution of plausible sharp images and kernels consistent with the observed blurry data and prior knowledge.

ideas.title: Bayesian Reconstruction via Learned Forward Likelihood and Implicit Prior
ideas.description: This system first learns an accurate forward conditional model p(low-res | high-res) to capture the complex, potentially stochastic degradation process. Simultaneously, it learns or incorporates an implicit prior distribution over high-resolution signals, for instance via a generative model like a GAN or normalizing flow. For a given low-resolution observation, the system performs inference by combining this learned likelihood and prior within a Markov Chain Monte Carlo or variational inference loop to sample from or approximate the true posterior p(high-res | low-res). This provides a robust, uncertainty-aware super-resolution method that directly addresses the inverse problem the original idea could not solve.

ideas.title: Apply Evolutionary Game Theory's Stability Concepts to GAN-Inspired Gibbs Model Training
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from Evolutionary Game Theory. The analogous problem is ensuring stable, robust outcomes in competitive co-evolutionary systems, which is solved formally using concepts like the Evolutionarily Stable Strategy (ESS). We adapt this solution to the context of using GAN training methods for fine-tuning a Gibbs model. The adapted idea proposes modifying the adversarial training framework by incorporating an ESS-inspired stability criterion into the loss function or update rule. This would guide the fine-tuning of the Gibbs model towards parameter states that are not just performant but also dynamically stable against perturbations in the model or its adversarial critic, leading to more reliable and robust convergence.

ideas.title: Non-Gibbs Probabilistic Models for Optimal Thumbnail Selection
ideas.description: This research direction investigates probabilistic models beyond Gibbs distributions, such as normalizing flows or energy-based models with non-exponential family potentials, to solve the conditional high-dimensional prediction task of generating optimal thumbnails from high-resolution images. The core problem is reframed not as a deterministic downscaling, but as learning a distribution over possible low-resolution summaries, where the 'best' thumbnail maximizes the preservation of semantic content and user engagement cues. This allows the model to capture complex, data-driven notions of importance that simple downsampling or Gibbs-based priors cannot, enabling the generation of thumbnails tailored for specific contexts or viewer preferences.

ideas.title: Conditional Generative Modeling via Tractable Likelihood Alternatives: A Systematic Comparison of Normalizing Flows and Diffusion Models to Gibbs Frameworks
ideas.description: This research conducts a systematic investigation into high-dimensional conditional prediction using probabilistic models based on tractable likelihood and simulation-free training, explicitly moving beyond the Gibbs distribution framework. It focuses on a controlled, empirical and theoretical comparison between modern alternatives—specifically normalizing flows and diffusion models—and traditional Gibbs-based energy-based models (EBMs) across key metrics: mode coverage, sampling speed, and conditional inference accuracy on standardized high-dimensional tasks. The goal is to rigorously map the trade-offs and failure modes of each paradigm, providing a principled guide for when to abandon the Gibbs formulation in favor of these alternative probabilistic architectures.

ideas.title: Adaptive Approximate Counting via Statistical Physics Sampling Methods
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that exact substructure counts are necessary for downstream graph learning tasks by drawing an analogy from statistical physics. In that field, exact enumeration of microstates is impossible for complex systems, so physicists rely on Monte Carlo sampling methods to approximate macroscopic observables with statistical guarantees. Adapting this solution, we propose to develop and analyze MCMC-based samplers that provide approximate, unbiased counts of graph substructures. The core investigation will assess whether these approximate counts, with quantifiable error bounds, preserve or even enhance utility in tasks like graph classification, property prediction, or anomaly detection, potentially trading precision for massive gains in scalability and computational efficiency.

ideas.title: Meta-Learning for Fast Adaptation in Inverse Substructure Generation

The core model learns to quickly adapt to new, unseen types of structural constraints (e.g., target counts for novel subgraph patterns) or graph degradations (e.g., missing nodes/edges, noise) from only a few examples. Instead of training a single model for one fixed substructure target, it acquires a prior over the space of graph generation tasks defined by substructure counts. This enables rapid few-shot adaptation to generate graphs satisfying novel combinatorial specifications or to reconstruct graphs from corrupted observations with unknown degradation patterns.

ideas.title: Non-Gibbs Generative Models for Inverse Substructure Targeting
ideas.description: This research direction develops novel probabilistic generative models for graphs that are not based on the Gibbs (exponential family) distribution framework, such as implicit models, diffusion models, or autoregressive models, specifically engineered for the inverse problem of generating graphs with targeted substructure counts. It moves beyond simple counting or likelihood-based generation by directly incorporating substructure count objectives into the model's learning or sampling process. This enables the controlled synthesis of graphs with desired high-order topological properties, which is difficult with traditional Gibbs models due to partition function intractability and limited expressivity for complex, global constraints. The outcome is a more powerful and flexible toolbox for graph generation in domains like drug discovery and network design, where specific functional motifs are required.

ideas.description: This idea inverts the standard conditional modeling direction in super-resolution by learning p(Low-Resolution | High-Resolution), analogous to 'perfect prognosis' methods in climate downscaling where coarse model outputs are statistically modeled conditioned on local high-resolution truth. The adapted solution trains a generative model to understand the manifold of possible low-resolution observations (e.g., different downsampling kernels, noise instances, sensor characteristics) that could arise from a single high-resolution source. For novel inference, this inverted model is used within a Bayesian framework to estimate the posterior distribution of high-resolution signals p(HR | LR), leading to a super-resolution method that explicitly accounts for the one-to-many ambiguity of the upscaling task and provides principled uncertainty estimates.

ideas.title: Adapting Modular Sensor Fusion from Robotics for Multi-Pathway CNN Statistics
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of modular sensor fusion from robotics to the problem of computing sufficient statistics with CNNs. In robotics, robust state estimation is achieved by processing data from heterogeneous sensors through specialized, parallel pathways before fusing them. Similarly, we propose to investigate a CNN architecture composed of multiple, specialized convolutional pathways, each designed to compute a distinct type of statistical feature from the input data (e.g., spatial, spectral, or textural moments). A learned fusion module, analogous to a sensor fusion center, would then integrate these diverse statistical summaries to form a more comprehensive and informative sufficient statistic, potentially improving performance in downstream tasks like inference, generation, or compression.

ideas.title: Scale up video super-resolution via statistical mechanics of turbulence
ideas.description: Extending a high-dimensional conditional model to the even higher-dimensional space of video frames faces a combinatorial explosion in complexity. Drawing an analogy from fluid dynamics, this idea adapts the Large Eddy Simulation (LES) solution for turbulence. In LES, the large, coherent structures are simulated directly, while the effects of innumerable small-scale eddies are statistically modeled. Adapted here, the video super-resolution model will explicitly learn and predict the dominant, temporally coherent structural and motion features across frames. The distribution of fine-scale, often stochastic, texture details—analogous to subgrid-scale turbulence—will be handled by a compact, conditional variational model that 'infills' plausible high-resolution pixels based on the resolved context. This hybrid explicit-implicit approach scales the problem by strategically allocating modeling capacity, making high-fidelity video super-resolution computationally tractable.

ideas.title: Uncertainty-Aware Meta-Learning with Generative Task Hallucination for Robust Adaptation to Unknown Degradations
ideas.description: This system enhances standard meta-learning for image restoration by embedding a test-time uncertainty quantification mechanism. When presented with a new degradation, the model first attempts a few gradient steps; high uncertainty in the adapted model's predictions flags the degradation as potentially out-of-distribution. A conditional generative adversarial network, pre-trained on a diverse set of synthetic corruptions, then hallucinates a distribution of related but in-distribution degradation tasks. The meta-learner adapts to these hallucinated tasks, which serve as a robust proxy, before final fine-tuning on the actual novel degradation, ensuring stable and effective adaptation even to unforeseen corruption types.

ideas.title: Incorporate a Global Workspace from Cognitive Science into CNN Architecture
ideas.description: Inspired by Global Workspace Theory from cognitive science, which solves the integration of specialized, modular brain processes via a central broadcasting system, this idea adapts that solution to computer vision. We propose augmenting a CNN with a parallel Global Workspace module that continuously integrates compressed feature information from across the spatial extent of an image. This module uses an attention mechanism to form a coherent global context vector, which is then broadcast back to and fused with local convolutional feature maps. This architectural innovation enables the network to handle long-range dependencies in image structure by allowing any local computation to be dynamically informed by the integrated understanding of the whole scene, much like cognitive integration in the brain.

ideas.title: Bias-Corrected Variational Likelihood Estimation via Adaptive Importance Sampling
ideas.description: This method combines the efficiency of variational inference with the asymptotic unbiasedness of Monte Carlo estimation to produce reliable likelihood approximations. A flexible variational family is trained to approximate the true posterior, then this distribution is used as the initial proposal for an adaptive annealed importance sampling (AIS) routine. The AIS bridge sequences and Markov chain transition kernels are themselves parameterized and optimized to minimize the variance of the final importance weights, ensuring stable and sample-efficient estimation. The final output is a statistically consistent estimate of the log-likelihood, suitable for rigorous model selection and evaluation.

ideas.title: Mobile Super-Resolution via Metabolic Pathway Pruning
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'pathway pruning' approach from metabolic engineering to create a lightweight super-resolution model for mobile devices. In metabolic engineering, complex biological networks are simplified by removing non-essential enzymes and pathways while maintaining core production capabilities through optimized alternative routes. Similarly, we propose to systematically analyze and prune redundant or computationally expensive operations in super-resolution neural networks, identifying which components are essential for mobile-relevant image enhancement tasks. The adapted solution involves developing an automated pruning algorithm that removes non-critical layers, neurons, and connections while introducing efficient alternative operations (like depthwise separable convolutions or attention mechanisms) to maintain performance. This creates a simplified, efficient 'metabolic pathway' for image super-resolution that runs in real-time on mobile hardware with minimal quality loss, analogous to how engineered microbial strains maintain production with reduced metabolic burden.

ideas.title: Multi-Scale Sparse Visual Attention with Adaptive Tokenization
ideas.description: This redesigned system introduces a computationally efficient attention mechanism for CNNs by first adaptively tokenizing the feature map into a reduced set of semantic patches. A lightweight gating network dynamically prunes irrelevant attention connections, enforcing sparsity. Attention is then computed hierarchically across multiple feature scales within the CNN pyramid, allowing the model to capture both fine-grained local structure and global context without the quadratic cost of dense pixel-wise attention.

ideas.title: Adapting Spin Glass Renormalization Group Methods for Local Relational Pooling
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to further develop and theoretically analyze the local relational pooling (LRP) method for substructure counting by drawing an analogy from statistical physics, specifically the study of spin glasses. The analogous problem is understanding complex, disordered systems with many interacting components. Physicists solve this using Renormalization Group (RG) methods, which involve coarse-graining local clusters of spins into effective 'super-spins' to derive scale-dependent effective theories while preserving essential statistical relationships. We adapt this solution by integrating the RG's iterative coarse-graining philosophy into the LRP framework. The adapted idea is to use the LRP's local, permutation-sensitive aggregations not as a single layer, but as the fundamental operation for building a hierarchical, multi-scale model of a graph. By theoretically analyzing how this iterative relational pooling transforms the statistical distribution of substructures—akin to tracking how an RG flow transforms an effective Hamiltonian—we can develop a principled understanding of LRP's expressivity, stability, and ability to perform substructure counting at multiple resolutions. This cross-disciplinary approach aims to provide a strong theoretical foundation for scaling and analyzing relational pooling architectures.

ideas.title: Meta-Learning with a Library of Degradation-Specific Adaptation Protocols
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from immunology, where the adaptive immune system rapidly generates a targeted response to new pathogens by leveraging a pre-existing library of immune cells, this idea adapts that solution to image restoration. The core method involves meta-learning not just a single set of adaptable weights, but a structured library or manifold of degradation-specific adaptation strategies (e.g., small network modules or optimization trajectories). During meta-testing on a novel corruption or scaling type, the model performs a fast inner-loop search to select and minimally refine the most analogous strategy from its library. This approach aims to achieve faster and more data-efficient adaptation to new degradations than standard model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML) by providing a richer, more structured prior knowledge base, directly inspired by biological immune memory and clonal selection.

ideas.title: Task-Adaptive Discovery of Predictive Relational Motifs with Meta-GNNs
ideas.description: This research designs a meta-GNN framework that learns to identify and leverage task-specific relational patterns beyond predefined substructures. Instead of approximating theoretical counting limits, the system employs adaptive message-passing and differentiable pooling mechanisms to discover which local graph properties are predictive for a given application. The approach is validated on real-world problems where success depends on flexible structural reasoning, not substructure counts, ensuring practical utility and robustness across diverse domains.

ideas.title: Context-Aware Dynamic Neural Engine for Robust Mobile Super-Resolution
ideas.description: This research proposes a dynamic neural architecture for mobile super-resolution that overcomes hardware heterogeneity by adapting in real-time. A lightweight meta-controller analyzes the device's current computational budget, thermal state, and battery constraints to generate optimized weights for a minimal core SR network. This approach ensures reliable, real-time performance by tailoring model complexity to the immediate operational context, rather than deploying a one-size-fits-all simplified model. The system maintains a single, tiny meta-model but can instantiate a wide range of efficient, context-specific networks for robust deployment on any mobile device.

ideas.title: Analyzing k-WL's finite-depth expressivity via statistical physics phase transitions
ideas.description: This research adapts methods from statistical physics, specifically percolation theory's analysis of finite-depth exploration limits, to investigate the limitations of k-WL with a bounded number of iterations for substructure counting. The analogous problem involves determining long-range connectivity in random networks using only local probes of limited depth, which is solved by studying the decay of correlation functions and identifying critical thresholds. The adapted idea applies this correlation decay framework to the iterative color refinement process of k-WL. By modeling how information about graph substructures propagates and diminishes across a fixed number of iterations, we aim to formally characterize which substructures (e.g., specific cycles or cliques of given sizes) become provably indistinguishable. This would yield theoretical bounds on the required iteration depth for various counting tasks and potentially identify 'phase transitions' in k-WL's expressive power as a function of iteration count and graph parameters.

ideas.title: Continuous Feature Subgraph Counting via Signal Processing Kernels
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from signal processing and computer vision to address substructure counting with continuous node/edge features. In computer vision, object detection operates directly on continuous pixel intensities using kernel methods and convolutional networks without discretization. Similarly, we propose developing kernel-based counting functions that measure continuous similarity between node/edge feature vectors instead of requiring exact discrete matches. This approach would enable substructure detection in graphs with rich, continuous features by using learned embeddings and similarity thresholds, moving beyond traditional discrete attribute assumptions in graph pattern matching.

ideas.title: Adaptive Learning Rate Scheduling via Chemical Reaction Network Principles
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from chemical engineering, where reaction rates in a reactor are dynamically optimized using feedback control of parameters like temperature and catalyst concentration to ensure stable, efficient production, this idea proposes a novel adaptive learning rate scheduler. The analogous solution involves continuous monitoring and adjustment to prevent instability (runaway reactions) or inefficiency (stagnation). Adapted to machine learning, the training algorithm would incorporate a controller that treats metrics like gradient variance and loss curvature as signals analogous to product yield and byproduct formation. This controller would dynamically adjust the learning rate and optimizer momentum in real-time, leading to more stable convergence and better computational performance than static or pre-programmed decay schedules.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Bayesian Modeling of Non-Stationary Textures for Adaptive Variance Reduction
ideas.description: This research introduces a hierarchical Bayesian model to formally address texture non-stationarity for variance reduction tasks. The core idea is to represent a non-stationary texture as being generated from a family of stationary processes, where the governing parameters vary smoothly across the spatial domain according to a latent random field. The system performs joint inference on this latent parameter field and the underlying texture statistics, allowing for adaptive pooling of information across regions with similar statistical properties. This provides a rigorous, data-driven mechanism to reduce variance without the extreme bias or data hunger of an unconstrained non-stationary model, leading to more robust texture analysis and synthesis.

ideas.title: Perceptually-Guided Adversarial Learning for Audio Super-Resolution
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel audio super-resolution system that directly addresses the perceptual shortcomings of standard methods. By integrating a learned perceptual audio metric and a temporal coherence module into an adversarial training framework, the model is optimized not just for spectral accuracy but for human-judged sound quality. This approach ensures the generated high-resolution audio is free from common artifacts like metallic ringing or temporal instability. The system is evaluated through both objective metrics and rigorous human listening studies across diverse audio domains, including speech, music, and environmental sounds.

ideas.title: Modeling Textures as Locally Stationary Processes via Adaptive Windowing
ideas.description: This idea challenges the core stationarity assumption in texture modeling by drawing an analogy from geophysics, where seismic signals are inherently non-stationary. The analogous solution uses adaptive time-frequency representations (like STFT) to enforce local stationarity within short windows. Adapted to texture analysis, the research would develop a framework to model textures as locally stationary random fields. This involves segmenting the texture image into spatially adaptive regions or using a multi-scale filter bank, within which a stationary model is fitted. The key innovation is a meta-model that describes the spatial evolution or transition of these local stationary parameters, thereby relaxing the global stationarity assumption for more accurate modeling and variance reduction in heterogeneous textures.

ideas.title: Uncertainty-Guided Sequential Degradation Estimation and Restoration
ideas.description: This redesigned system first employs a lightweight estimator network to predict the noise parameters and blur kernel present in the low-resolution input. Using these estimates, a conditional denoising module processes the image, simultaneously producing a denoised intermediate and a pixel-wise uncertainty map. This uncertainty map then gates and guides a subsequent super-resolution network, allowing it to focus its capacity on reconstructing high-frequency details in confident regions while applying restrained, uncertainty-aware upsampling in problematic areas. This sequential, conditioned approach prevents task interference and improves robustness to real-world, unknown degradation mixtures.

The model is optimized for a Pareto frontier of objectives, allowing users or downstream systems to select the thumbnail that best balances clarity, appeal, and relevance for a given use case.

ideas.title: Temporal Graph Motif Counting via Time-Series Decomposition
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that substructure counting must be applied to static graph aggregates. Drawing an analogy from signal processing, where complex time-series are decomposed into intrinsic mode functions for analysis, we propose adapting this solution to temporal graphs. The analogous solution involves breaking down a non-stationary signal into simpler oscillatory components to track how its features evolve. Adapted to graph theory, this means developing a method to decompose a dynamic graph's edge sequence into a set of fundamental 'temporal graph modes'—each representing a coherent phase of structural activity. The core research idea is to then perform motif (substructure) counting not on the whole timeline, but within and across these temporal modes. This adaptation would yield new metrics, such as motif birth/death rates, motif persistence within a mode, and motif correlations across successive modes, fundamentally extending substructure analysis to capture the dynamic narrative of network evolution.

ideas.title: Mobile-Optimized Graph Generation for Target Subgraph Profiles
ideas.description: This research develops a lightweight, mobile-optimized generative model that inverts the substructure counting problem. Instead of a heavy model analyzing a complex graph, this efficient network takes a target profile (e.g., 'generate a graph with 5 triangular motifs and 10 star-shaped subgraphs') and produces a plausible graph satisfying those constraints. The core innovation is achieving this conditional generation with the computational and memory footprint suitable for real-time use on mobile or edge devices. This enables novel applications like on-device molecular scaffold suggestion for field chemists, personalized local network synthesis for privacy-aware social apps, or dynamic procedural content generation in mobile games based on desired interaction patterns.

ideas.title: Incremental, Event-Driven Maintenance of Temporal Subgraph Counts
ideas.description: This research develops algorithms for maintaining accurate counts of small subgraphs (e.g., triangles, wedges) in a dynamically evolving graph stream. Instead of periodic full-graph analysis, the system processes a stream of edge addition and deletion events, updating all relevant substructure counts in an incremental, near-real-time manner. The core innovation is a set of differential counting rules and compact data structures that track only the local neighborhoods involved in each change, ensuring scalability. This enables efficient temporal motif analysis and anomaly detection in high-throughput, continuously changing networks like social media or financial transaction graphs.
